title: How Italy’s Next Great White Wine Came Back from the Dead
summary:
The rise of Derthona was driven by bold viticulture and smart marketing, transforming a near-extinct Piedmont grape into a flagship wine with DOC dreams.

The post How Italy’s Next Great White Wine Came Back from the Dead appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Winemakers in Piedmont have longed for an indigenous white varietal with enough cachet to match the grandeur of Barolo and Barbaresco. Their prayers have been answered by Timorasso. Resurrected from the valleys of Colli Tortonesi, 50 miles east of Alba, it makes a sharp yet silky wine with an electric intensity and serious aging potential.

Forty years ago, there were zero commercial bottlings of Timorasso. But a curious vigneron with fewer than 400 vines set out to change that. Today, there are over 500 hectares of Timorasso, 117 producers (and counting), and top bottles are selling for over $100. None of this was an accident.

Resuscitating a Region

Timorasso was established in Piedmont by the 14th century. In 1469, Leonardo da Vinci served it at the wedding of Isabella of Aragon, his patron. The grape variety covered 2,000 hectares—one quarter of the region’s vineyards—until its demise from phylloxera in the late 19th century. The finicky grape was too costly and labor intensive to replant for such meager returns. The Colli Tortonesi DOC, established in 1973, didn’t even include Timorasso until 2005.

Walter Massa, a native of Colli Tortonesi, took over his family’s winery in the 1970s and quickly outgrew their provincial ambitions. In 1987, he bottled wine from 400 scattered Timorasso vines. The results were underwhelming, but upon revisiting those bottles a couple of years later, he found they’d transformed into something unique and delicious.

Massa gathered cuttings from forgotten parcels in the region’s valleys for a Massal-selection Timorasso vineyard. He convinced a handful of neighbors to cultivate it, including Claudio Mariotto, Piercarlo and Elisa Semino of La Colombera, and Luigi Boveri, and they created a consortium to establish quality standards and promote the grape.

Enrico Mandirola’s family joined them in the early ’90s, purchasing a vineyard planted in 1920 by a family who’d kept it for personal use. Today, his Tantéi bottling is made from the oldest Timorasso vines on the planet.

But the consortium soon discovered why the grape had been abandoned a hundred years prior. The vine over-buds, struggles to flower, and has large, tight bunches prone to mildew and sunburn, requiring constant work for unpredictable yields. It took three times the work for one-third of the fruit compared to other grapes in the region. Without premium pricing, it was financially ruinous. By 2003, they’d reached 60 hectares, then stalled.

Another hurdle was marketing: Calling the wine Colli Tortonesi, given its reputation as a simple red wine region, wouldn’t cut it. Massa’s next stroke of genius was coining Derthona, the Latin name for the city of Tortona. He allowed anyone making 100% Timorasso with a year of aging to use the term. He wasn’t just reviving a grape; he was resuscitating a region.

The Barolo Invasion

In 2014, Luca Roagna and his father, Alfredo, were offered an incredible opportunity, marked by sadness. Alfredo’s dear friend died suddenly, leaving his wife to tend to a hectare of old Timorasso vines. Luca, a perfectionist, worried they couldn’t dedicate enough time to make wines up to the standards of their Roagna winery. They took the risk anyway, with Alfredo committing himself to the vineyard. Their Derthona became an instant classic—with a Barolo price tag.

Massa saw an opportunity. If established Barolo producers came to Colli Tortonesi, their stamp of approval, technical knowledge, and global distribution could put Derthona in every collector’s hands, validating his life’s work.

He texted Andrea Farinetti, head of Borgogno, at 3 a.m.: “Come buy a vineyard.” By 10 a.m. that day, Farinetti was at Massa’s house. He was treated to a full tour of Colli Tortonesi—every vigneron and Timorasso barrel, plus salumi makers, Volpedo peach growers, and Montébore cheesemakers. Massa insisted that Farinetti understand the entire region. They finished the tour at midnight, and Farinetti bought the vineyard.

His first vintage, in 2015, was a test run for the family. The following year, he had an incredibly rare opportunity in the wine world—to start from zero in a virgin market and shape perceptions rather than labor to change them.

A thousand bottles were produced, each hand-labeled, in its own box, and shipped as a gift to his top customers, with a handwritten note explaining who Massa is and that next year they would be offered a six-bottle allocation at the same price as his Barolo. Not one person refused the 2016 vintage.

At a Barolo industry dinner in 2017, Farinetti brought the only white wine: a magnum of his Derthona. The table included the producers Viberti, Pio Cesare, Vietti, and La Spinetta. Everyone left with Massa’s number, and they all make Derthona now.

The arrival of deep-pocketed Barolo producers in Colli Tortonesi drove up land prices. Some locals saw it as validation; others were resentful of outsiders capitalizing on their hard work and the rapidly changing market. Most acknowledge that Timorasso’s future depends on both.

What Are We Drinking?

The calcareous marl of the Apennine foothills infuses Derthona wines with a cutting minerality. Winemaking techniques are still evolving, but most producers agree that the grape despises oxygen and oak treatments. Experiments abound: cement tanks, skin contact in amphora, bâttonage, 100% reductive methods, and hyperoxidation.

In their youth, Derthona wines can be peachy and floral or stark and reductive, with a salty signature. After three to five years in the bottle, they develop notes of hay, cream, toasted hazelnuts, acacia, beeswax, turmeric, ginger, and fennel. For those who can’t wait, decanting is necessary.

On the Mount Olympus of Wine

Derthona is a candidate to become a DOC, and if it does, it will have the strictest guidelines for any Italian white wine: bottle-weight limits for sustainability and extended maturation times of six, 12, and 18 months for Piccolo, Derthona, and Riserva classifications, respectively.

Vineyards need three to seven years to produce fruit, and rapid plantings mean hundreds of hectares will come online over the next few vintages. Growers project two million bottles in 2027. Cuttings have also snuck outside of Piedmont into the hands of Idlewild in Sonoma and Beykush Winery in Ukraine. The next challenge for Derthona is to keep the wine’s inimitable features at the fore and ensure the quality level remains high.

What makes this story remarkable is that this historic region has had the chance to start anew. It took Meursault and Rheingau centuries to build their reputations; Timorasso is attempting the same in 40 years.

When Walter Massa was asked whether there was anything else he wanted to share about Timorasso, he responded, “Grapes are like great men. Once understood and satisfied, they will be faithful friends. Derthona is now on the Mount Olympus of world wine, in the cellars of all the rich in the world, on the palates of all men of goodwill.”

title: Italy’s Best-Kept Wine Travel Secret Is a Quick Jaunt from Venice
summary:
After landing at Venice's Marco Polo Airport, why not head east instead? Postpone your gondola ride, rent a car, and cross into Friuli-Venezia Giulia, a lesser-visited region full of culinary gems.

The post Italy’s Best-Kept Wine Travel Secret Is a Quick Jaunt from Venice appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
When you land at Venice Marco Polo Airport, you instinctively want to hop on a water taxi and disappear into the magic of Venice. But what if you go east instead? I suggest you postpone your gondola ride, rent a car, and cross into Friuli-Venezia Giulia, a lesser-visited region full of culinary gems.

There are no anchor cities here, like Florence in Tuscany or Bologna in Emilia-Romagna. Instead, you’ll find quiet river towns, stretches of forest, hilly vineyards, and artisan families who have been making things for generations: knives, cheese, honey, prosciutto. Some stops on this Friuli itinerary feel like the edge of the known world. Others, like Trieste or Udine, are bustling cities where empires once clashed and cultures have been intertwining and negotiating for centuries.

“What excited me about Friuli [wine] was that everyone had a completely different take,” says Bobby Stuckey, Master Sommelier and co-founder of Frasca Food and Wine in Boulder, Colorado, who has been taking trips to the region for 30 years. “In Napa, you might find everyone working with the same consultant. In Friuli, everyone was doing something different, and they were all friends. That spirit never left.”

Sacile and San Daniele del Friuli

Your first stop, just inside Friuli’s western edge, is the town of Sacile. Walk along the river and order your first glass of Friulano, a dry white wine with citrus peel and almond notes. Nothing feels urgent, and that is the point.

To best understand Friuli, “Start with the people who make things,” says Elena Orzan, a local cultural ambassador. Head north to Maniago, where a knife- and blade-making tradition has continued for centuries and remains very much alive. Drive farther and discover the mosaics of Spilimbergo, assembled piece by piece over the centuries.

The road climbs toward San Daniele del Friuli, where La Bottega del Prosciutto di Levi, open since 1890, sells salumi, smoked ricotta from the Carnic Alps, local cheeses, and wines. Order prosciutto of different ages alongside a glass of Friulano from the tap and let the afternoon slow to a stop.

Before you leave, step into the Biblioteca Guarneriana, founded in 1466; its shelves hold 12,000 antique books and 600 manuscript codices, including a 14th-century handwritten copy of The Divine Comedy. Nearby, the Church of Sant’Antonio Abate is adorned with frescoes so stunning that it is known as the Sistine Chapel of Friuli.

WINE: Friulano Grave DOC, Pinot Grigio Ramato, Cividin

FOOD: Prosciutto di San Daniele, frico, ricotta affumicata

NOT TO MISS: Biblioteca Guarneriana, Church of Sant’Antonio Abate, Scuola Mosaicisti del Friuli

Tarcento, Savorgnano del Torre, and Nimis

Head into the foothills, where the land lifts and folds, vines appear in pockets framed by forest, and the mountains loom just beyond. In Tarcento, younger growers are returning to old vineyard sites and working with the Refosco dal Peduncolo Rosso grape with newfound precision. A short drive brings you to the village of Savorgnano del Torre, where the wines lean on Friulano and Picolit, which is more famous as a sweet variety, but here it’s dry, driven, fresh, and electric.

Continue higher to Nimis, home to the sweet wine Ramandolo, one of Friuli’s quiet revelations that’s defined by its structure, tension, and a deep connection to the land. Stop at the winery Dario Coos for a tasting that reframes everything you thought you knew about the variety.

“Friuli’s strength is that no one is trying to make the same wine,” says Lachlan Mackinnon-Patterson, co-founder of Frasca Food and Wine and Benvenusa Imports. “That independence is what keeps it alive.”

WINE: Ramandolo DOCG (Verduzzo), Savorgnano del Torre Bianco, Refosco dal Peduncolo Rosso

FOOD: pitina, Prosciutto di Sauris, strucolo de pomi

NOT TO MISS: Castello di Tarcento, Valli del Torre, Castello di Coia

Udine

In Udine’s architecture, you see Venice in the façades, but sense something else in the buildings themselves—Habsburg order and spacing, a rhythm that’s distinctly Central European. Walk up to the castle for a view that reveals the whole region: the plains stretching west, the hills rising to the east, the mountains cutting the northern horizon.

Come down into the Palazzo Arcivescovile, where Tiepolo painted some of his most luminous early frescoes. Then find a table. The real magic of Udine happens at street level, in the aperitivo hour that begins around 5 p.m. and keeps no fixed schedule. A glass of Friulano, a crostino, a conversation already in progress. This is not performance; this is how the city works.

On Saturdays, the spot to hit is Osteria al Cappello. Stuckey says it’s essential, the kind of restaurant where you immediately feel part of city life, rather than watching it from the outside.

WINE: Pinot Bianco, Malvasia Istriana, Tazzelenghe

FOOD: frico, crostini con lardo, musetto con brovada

NOT TO MISS: Casa Cavazzini modern and contemporary art museum, Castello di Udine, Piazza Libertà

Cividale del Friuli

Cividale was founded as Forum Julii, possibly by Julius Caesar, around 50 B.C.E., and later became the first capital of the Lombard duchy, giving the entire region its name. The Ponte del Diavolo bridge spans the gorge with drama that stops you in your tracks. The Tempietto Longobardo, an eighth-century chapel tucked into the riverbank, features carved figures that have survived intact for 13 centuries. Cividale is not a footnote; it is where the story of Friuli begins.

Find a table for cjarsons, the local stuffed pasta filled with herbs, ricotta, and sometimes a touch of chocolate or cinnamon. It’s a recipe that’s been carried across the centuries and made differently in every kitchen.

WINE: Schioppettino di Prepotto, Rosazzo Bianco, Pignolo

FOOD: cjarsons, gubana, trota del Natisone

NOT TO MISS: Piazza Paolo Diacono, Duomo di Santa Maria Assunta, Rosazzo Abbey

Cormòns, Collio, and Oslavia

Head south toward Cormòns and into Collio, where you’ll be greeted by vines in every direction and soft light. As you move through the landscape, the place names shift and the language on the signs softens. Without realizing it, you have crossed into Brda, Slovenia. Producers like Edi Keber work on both sides of the border, not as a statement, but because it has always been this way.

“The diversity within such a small stamp of land is hard to describe,” says Giampaolo Venica of the winery Venica and Venica, which is known for its single-vineyard Sauvignon Blanc. Friulian Sauvignon Blanc sits between the minerality and herbal notes of great Sancerre and the punchy fruit of New Zealand Sauvignon Blanc—a mix of crushed stone, fresh herbs, and white grapefruit with an acidity that defines what “racy” means in wine.

Stay at La Subida, an eco-friendly resort run by the Sirk family. Polenta arrives from a copper pot, churned for hours and cut by a string as tradition demands. Visits turn into conversations, conversations turn into corks being pulled, and lifelong friendships are made. “They don’t have the pressure of tourism,” says Bobby Stuckey of the region. “They are genuinely excited that you are there.”

Then find your way to Oslavia, 200 meters from the Slovenian border, where a war memorial above the vines holds the remains of more than 57,000 soldiers who fell here in World War I. What grew back was wine. The village has seven producers, including Gravner, Radikon, Fiegl, and Dario Prinčič. In the late 1990s, Gravner and Radikon returned to ancient techniques, skin maceration, and amphoras buried in the earth. What the world called orange wine was, in Oslavia, simply a return to their legacy.

WINE: Collio Bianco, Ribolla Gialla macerata, Collio Rosso

FOOD: polenta tagliata, D’Osvaldo prosciutto, frtalja

NOT TO MISS: Sacrario Militare di Oslavia, La Subida, Route of the Orange Benches

Gorizia and Carso

Gorizia tells its story without raising its voice. For decades, it was two cities: Gorizia in Italy and Nova Gorica in what was then Yugoslavia (now Slovenia), with a wall dividing them during the Cold War. Today, you can walk across Piazza della Transalpina, a shared square that straddles the border.

Continue south into Carso, or Kras, where limestone dominates the thin soil and rock must be broken to give the vines a chance. What comes back are wines of clarity and tension: white Vitovska and red Terrano. Find an osmiza, a temporary farm tavern marked by a branch outside when it’s open, like the one at Zidarich winery, and sit down at a long table for wine, salumi, and cheese. “The real magic of Friuli isn’t found in its landmarks,” says Isabella Lupi of Amandum winery. “It’s in people’s homes, around the table.”

WINE: Vitovska, Terrano del Carso

FOOD: jota (or iota), goulash alla goriziana, Kraški pršut

NOT TO MISS: Piazza della Transalpina, Castello di Gorizia, Grotta Gigante

Trieste

Finally, you arrive in Trieste, and everything converges. It was once the Habsburg Empire’s great Adriatic port, a crossroads where merchants, sailors, writers, and refugees coexisted. The café culture they cultivated remains vibrant, from Caffè degli Specchi and Caffè San Marco to the old Viennese-style coffeehouses that line the main avenue, each with its own regulars and rituals. In Trieste, a café is not a stop on the way to somewhere else; it is the destination.

The novelist Italo Svevo, born and buried here, gave the city its literary soul. Its identity has never quite settled, with history always pressing in and beauty and melancholy appearing as the same thing seen from different angles. James Joyce felt it, too, living here for many years. Two of the 20th century’s great literary minds independently chose to live in the same small city on the edge of a crumbling empire, so something special was clearly happening there. Stand in Piazza Unità d’Italia, which opens onto the Adriatic, and you’ll understand it.

The food scene is as layered as the history; don’t miss the storied buffets where pork cuts have been carved and served by weight for over a century. Go to Karaktér for seafood and natural wine and to Pagna Panificio Artigianale for sourdough and a bottle list that spans the Old and New Worlds.

Walking through Trieste, you begin to recognize everything you have experienced in the region: Slovenian influence, Italian pace, the weight of history, the openness of the sea. This is the sum of Friuli-Venezia Giulia. When people leave, they ask the same question: Why don’t I live here? Friuli doesn’t offer a good answer. It just waits for you to return.

WINE: Malvasia del Carso, Terrano Rosato, Carso Bianco

FOOD: bollito di maiale con kren, sardoni in savor, putizza

NOT TO MISS: Miramare Castle, Piazza Unità d’Italia, Museo Revoltella

title: ICYMI: Smoked Dessert Wine Exists, and It’s Great
summary:
Umbrian farmers are reviving vinosanto da uva affumicata, a smoked dessert wine that was once on the brink of extinction.

The post ICYMI: Smoked Dessert Wine Exists, and It’s Great appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
The dogs are going crazy.

They trot behind Chiara Filippi, greeting the stranger who has just arrived. Filippi shakes my hand and introduces her husband, Claudio Ceccarelli, who has just returned from the forest with truffles dug up by their three yellow labs.

It’s an early-November morning in Umbria. Mist clings to the golden leaves that blanket the region’s rolling hills, like wisps of smoke rising from the valley. It’s the offseason, but there’s no downtime at an agriturismo like La Miniera di Galparino. Even if Filippi’s rooms aren’t booked, there’s work to be done on the farm: feeding the chickens, tending the olive groves, and checking on the grapes hanging above the hearth.

Since 2000, Filippi and Ceccarelli have run this 120-acre farmstay in the Upper Tiber Valley, near the historic walled city of Città di Castello. For even longer, they’ve been producing vinosanto da uva affumicata, a sweet wine made from smoked grapes, carrying on a tradition passed down by Filippi’s father. You’ll only find this wine here, in the heart of Italy’s tobacco country.

I was curious how smoke, something usually considered a flaw, could be a defining characteristic of their wine. After five minutes on the farm—seeing the vines of a nearly extinct grape variety, the willow branches they use to tie grape bunches, and the roses planted in the vineyard to be an early warning for disease—I started to see the bigger picture.

When Filippi and Ceccarelli began bottling it, vinosanto da uva affumicata had nearly disappeared. It was too time consuming to make and impossible to scale. La Miniera di Galparino’s is a blend of Trebbiano Toscano and three other local varieties, and they only produce about 150 bottles a year. But thanks to the nonprofit organization Slow Food, 11 families in the valley are receiving support to keep producing it, even if the commercial prospects are practically non-existent. Now, this tradition, tied to the region’s tobacco industry, has a future that seemed uncertain until recently.

Tobacco Fields and Kitchen Fires

Italy is Europe’s largest tobacco producer, and Umbria is one of the country’s leading sources. For about 450 years, not long after it was brought to Europe from the Americas, tobacco has been grown, harvested, and cured here, especially around Città di Castello. Driving around the region with Sergio Consigli, the head of Slow Food Umbria, the crop’s staggering value becomes clear. At one point, Consigli starts crying out, “Bancomat! [ATM]” every time we pass a tobacco field. I lose count of how many times he says it.

Historically, though, Umbrian tobacco farmers were not wealthy. Most had to dry tobacco in their farmhouses. In the same spaces, they also produced vinosanto (“holy wine”), traditionally made by drying grapes for months until they become raisins.

Without another room to devote solely to winemaking, Umbrian families would hang tobacco leaves and grapes together above their kitchen hearths. “They were peasants. They had to make use of whatever space they had,” Filippi tells me.

As they dried, the grapes would absorb the aroma of the tobacco leaves, as well as what Consigli calls arrosto, the smoke of whatever was cooked in the kitchen. The process yielded a wine unlike the vinosanto made on the other side of the hills in Tuscany. “This,” says Consigli, a proud Umbrian, “is only here—nowhere else on Earth.”

The Taste of Smoke

With Slow Food’s assistance, the production of vinosanto da uva affumicata has now been codified. The grapes must be strung up and naturally influenced by smoke, and the wine must age in barrels no bigger than 100 liters for at least five years.

Under the direction of Filippi and Ceccarelli’s son, Nicola, La Miniera di Galparino ages their wines for 10 years. The extra time gives them a character unlike any I’ve tasted.

Each of their wines is sweet and has a rich mahogany color. Before I taste it, Consigli instructs me to shut my eyes, cup my hands around the glass, and draw a deep breath. “After that, you’ll tell me, ‘It’s amazing!’ and I’ll say, ‘I know,’” he says. Whiffs of peat and tobacco layer with the fig, honey, and salted caramel flavors on my palate. Then we open a 20-year-old bottle. Its flavor is so profound that even Ceccarelli, who helped make the stuff, is nearly brought to tears.

After the tasting, I’m led to an old stable the couple converted into a kitchen for guests. Dangling from the rafters are the coppiole (couples), the neatly tied bunches of grapes that make up the wine. About 80% of it is Trebbiano; the rest is a mix of Dolciame Bianco, Grechetto, and Malfiore, a variety that was on the verge of extinction when they found it growing on the property. The family is now helping to revive it with support from the University of Perugia.

“Vinosanto grapes are always picked first,” Ceccarelli explains. “They need the right amount of space between the fruit so that air passes through and they don’t mold.”

After harvest, tradition dictates you tie them up with willow branches. In January or February, after pressing, the must is then put in barrels with the most important ingredient, the mother yeast, which stirs back into action when the weather warms up in spring and summer.

Like sourdough starters, each winemaking family here has its own mother yeast. Some, like La Miniera di Galparino’s, are more than 100 years old. “The mother is everything to vinosanto,” says Filippi. “In it, you taste the flavors of the seasons and a sense of the past.”

A Bottle at Birth, a Bottle at Marriage

After the tasting, I drive across the valley to meet farmer Davide Morganti. He calls his vinosanto Bubbola after the hoopoe, a migratory bird seen as a harbinger of spring. Morganti releases about 500 bottles a year, selling many to local restaurants.

He says making wine is as much an excuse to be with his family as it is to make money. After 15 years in a factory, Morganti came home 25 years ago to make wine with his father. His brother designs the labels. In their free time, they hunt for truffles and wild asparagus. “It’s a better life,” he tells me.

“Every farmer here has made vinosanto at least once,” Morganti explains as we savor a 10-year-old wine that smells just like the Umbrian countryside in autumn. “It’s a drink for celebrations.”

In a time before tractors, farmers would treat their neighbors to vinosanto and cake in return for helping bring their harvest to market. “We also make vinosanto the year our children are born. When they get married, we drink that year’s bottle,” he adds. “Vinosanto is the identity of this place. It’s important we keep making it as best we can.”

Back at La Miniera di Galparino, over a meal of steak drizzled with homemade olive oil and risotto with wild porcini mushrooms, the wine’s role in the connective tissue of the valley becomes even clearer. “Vinosanto was born because poor people wanted to have a precious thing to give to guests,” Filippi says. “We were poor people making something rich.”

They’re not peasant farmers anymore, but that instinct to turn what they have into something worth sharing hasn’t gone anywhere. In today’s world, where everything has been optimized, she believes a wine like this can still be a gesture of hospitality and a regional icon.

“This used to be a place you’d pass through,” Filippi says. The resurgence of vinosanto might give the valley a flagship product—and with it, she says, “valore, some value.”

title: Italy’s Smallest Wine Region Is a Valley Frozen in Time
summary:
Spanning just 450 hectares, Valle d'Aosta is rife with old-world charm and brisk alpine wines.

The post Italy’s Smallest Wine Region Is a Valley Frozen in Time appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
I stood in my Keds on the edge of a slushy snowbank, 8,500 feet up in the Italian Alps. A hundred yards away, a refuge hut with a chimney stack promised warmth and lunch. To say I was ill-prepared for this journey would be an understatement.

Crossing snowy peaks hadn’t been the plan when I left the hotel that morning. It was 75 degrees where we were standing. The receptionist said the trails were easy, well manicured, and fit for all ages. We’d reach the waterfalls within 45 minutes and then have lunch at the refuge hut. I thought my travel partner, Anthony, and I were taking a quick walk along a picturesque path through swaying grasses dotted with edelweiss blossoms. Nobody mentioned snow, proper footwear, or a jacket for my bare shoulders. All I packed was a bottle of Elio Ottin’s Fumin and some mocetta, the local cured beef.

As fast-walking New Yorkers, we blew through the waterfalls in half the suggested time. Pushing all the way to lunch now seemed like a no-brainer—until we saw backpackers carrying their skis up the switchback trail ahead of us. The air was thin, my back was sunburned, and Anthony disappeared ahead. Wild rams with curled horns stared me down from across the valley, daring me to keep going.

Two days earlier, we’d driven north from Piedmont into Valle d’Aosta, Italy’s smallest wine region. We followed the Dora Baltea river through the ancient Saint Bernard pass, the most important Alpine crossroad between Southern and Central Europe since pre-Roman times. Even traversing a modern highway felt like being shuttled to not just another place, but another time.

We passed medieval castles perched along bluffs, built by whomever controlled the valley at the time. Nestled between Europe’s highest peaks—Mont Blanc, Monte Rosa, and the Matterhorn—terraced vineyards and orchards were etched into the mountainsides. The scene was straight out of The Sound of Music.

We rolled into the town of Cogne as the sunset lit the jagged peaks above the Bellevue Hotel and Spa. The resort was rife with Old-World charm: herb gardens for the in-house restaurant, Renaissance frescoes on the lobby ceilings, antique furniture, and knick-knacks everywhere. Dinner was gooey raclette over potatoes and local steaks, paired beautifully with Ermes Pavese Blanc de Morgex et de la Salle—a white so brisk it could have come straight from glacier melt.

Wine Country, Alpine-Style

Valle d’Aosta is a tiny wine region—about 450 hectares planted, the same size as Zuccardi’s Santa Julia vineyard in Mendoza—and in some ways, Italy’s most versatile. The region’s wines had almost disappeared, only to be revived in the 1970s with a whopping 19 grape varieties, something to suit everyone.

There are a few you’ve heard of, like Gamay, Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, and Pinot Gris. The region’s Nebbiolo, locally called Picotendro, may be the original source of the more famous Piedmont wines. But there are many varieties you won’t find anywhere else and probably aren’t familiar with. The regal Petite Arvine is an excellent substitute for Chardonnay, Cornalin is angular and structured, and Mayolet is perfumed and silky.

Our timing was perfect: It was an open-cave day, so most wineries were welcoming visitors. Grosjean Vins, one of the larger family-owned estates, specializes in growing and bottling most of the indigenous varieties separately. With alpine freshness and mineral purity as a backbone, they are among the few sources exploring the region’s grapes side by side. Down the valley at the La Kiuva cooperative, with seven or eight families farming plots as small as 200 square meters, we tried their Nebbiolo-based red blend—light bodied, aromatic, fruity, and age-worthy.

Valle d’Aosta Grape Varieties

Petit Rouge

The “little red one” is indigenous to Valle d’Aosta and its most popular grape. It is occasionally labeled varietally, although it makes up the bulk of blends like Torrette and Enfer d’Arvier. The wines are full of juicy red-berry flavors, rich in body, and velvety in tannins.

Fumin

Bloody, sanguine wines with inky black juice and meaty, herbal flavors akin to Syrah from the Northern Rhône. They have astute tannins and a core of acidity that keeps them buoyant and fresh.

Prié Blanc

Also known as Blanc de Morgex, this grape soars to great heights, growing at elevations of nearly 4,000 feet—the highest in mainland Europe. The wines are airy and mineral, with bright acidity, making them great for sparkling wine.

Cornalin

Saved from extinction in the 1980s, this finicky grape makes mountain wine with notes of cracked pepper, tart berries, and wild herbs. With low tannin and laser acidity, it’s a delicate red, great served slightly chilled.

Mayolet

Tightly packed clusters make this grape difficult to grow, and it is rarely bottled on its own. Those who put in the effort are richly rewarded with intense aromatics of rose petals, mountain herbs, and sweet incense, along with juicy berry flavors and soft, silky tannins.

What Grows Together

Italy markets itself as an agriturismo experience, but most farms in the country are monocultures. Valle d’Aosta is different, with agricultural versatility being a defining feature. Only 1% of vineyards are bigger than a hectare. Crushed against mountainsides, mechanization is impossible. Farmers work by hand, forced to diversify to survive—grains, potatoes, and livestock can be found alongside the vines.

The old town of Aosta, still surrounded by ancient Roman walls, gets surprisingly hot daytime temperatures during the summer. Locals cultivate both Alpine and warm-climate fare—almonds, figs, and pistachios—alongside herbs like génépy, savory, and thyme, as well as cheese, apples, meat, and grains.

This creates the ultimate “what grows together, goes together” experience. Whites are paired with wild boar, reds alongside river fish. Fontina, aged in Alpine herbs, loves a dry, aromatic Muscat. In restaurants decorated with handmade plaid curtains, you’ll eat fresh-baked bread from local grains, potatoes in every form, meat they raised or hunted, all washed down with valley wines. A glass of génépy liqueur from a home still often follows, all served with genuine mountain hospitality.

Back on the Mountain

Standing at that snow bank, my stomach growled and my back burned. My Keds were soaked through. As uncomfortable as it would be to keep walking, I had to make it to the hut.

Once inside, we popped the Fumin. It was sanguine and meaty, with notes of alpine herbs and violets, lean acidity, and velvety tannins. After that trek, anything would’ve been welcome, but this was divine with our rustic pasta ragù.

Our socks dried by the woodstove. We polished off the wine, tied our shoes, and stepped back into the snow. We made it down the mountain with wet feet, singing the entire way.

title: In Tuscany’s ‘Wild West,’ Young Winemakers Embrace Adventure
summary:
Unburdened by the past, ambitious winemakers in the Maremma are carving out space for themselves.

The post In Tuscany’s ‘Wild West,’ Young Winemakers Embrace Adventure appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Castello di Montepò is a 14th-century Tuscan castle surrounded by almost 1,500 acres of untouched wilderness and carefully manicured vineyards—the kind of place you would call enchanting. Its primary inhabitant is Tancredi Biondi Santi, an exuberant 35-year-old whose name evokes a storied Tuscan pedigree. He cuts a fittingly princely figure, presiding over the vines that produce the majestic wines of this estate—from sophisticated but buoyant Sangiovese to textured, patrician cru Cabernet.

Biondi Santi is a leading winemaker in the southwest corner of Tuscany known as the Maremma, which resembles the setting of a fairy tale. But if the Maremma is a fairy tale, it’s very much a Cinderella story.

Though the Etruscans once cultivated wine here with great success, the intervening millennia erased most of that legacy. By the early 20th century, the Maremma was a swampy, mosquito-ridden mess. Malaria be damned, Mussolini and his fascist regime attempted to restore the land as part of their ongoing effort to render Italy agriculturally self-sufficient.

Despite the relative success of that undertaking, this subregion of Tuscany has always been an unlikely contender for viticultural greatness. But that hasn’t stopped a whole generation of Maremmani winemakers from hacking their way through the obstacles to carve out a space for themselves, despite the odds.

Referred to as Tuscany’s “Wild West,” the Maremma remains relatively untamed and infrequently visited by tourists. Its inhabitants haven’t necessarily tried to push back against that reputation. Hotel options are few, but one, the devastatingly charming Locanda Sospesa in the tiny town of Pereta, is worthy of a special trip. From the balcony of the boutique hotel, looking out over a breathtaking view of the valley, owner Johnny Petrucci said that while he and his wife, co-owner Elizabeth, love offering an intimate kind of hospitality, they struggle to attract visitors to the region. “It’s hard to get here, and no one is really invested in making it any easier,” he said.

“Maremma is called the Wild West of Tuscany for a very real reason,” said Ettore Rizzi, winemaker at Fattoria Le Pupille. “Long distances separate us, and nature dominates life.”

As a young (not yet 30) player in the territory, Rizzi has expanded upon the practices he learned from his mother, celebrated winemaker Elisabetta Geppetti. He balances precision with a penchant for taking risks— for example, he bottles a small amount of nutty, floral, and riotously herbaceous Petit Manseng from a minuscule parcel that essentially constitutes a clos. These risks have paid off, but not without a great deal of effort to convince both locals and the wine world at large of the region’s potential. “People [in the Maremma] are quite closed in terms of reevaluating or changing their philosophies and lifestyles,” Rizzi laments.

The Wild West nickname doesn’t quite capture the full reality of the Maremma. If anything, this dense, lush slice of Tuscany feels more like the Amazon, and the people who make wine here are better described as explorers in search of secrets than cowboys satisfied with a hot meal and banjo-strumming around a campfire.

This is especially true for Biondi Santi, whose family were once the uncontested stars of Montalcino before selling their historic estate and moving to the Maremma. “The combination of ancient viticultural roots and relatively recent agricultural redevelopment has created a culture that is both pragmatic and pioneering,” he said. “There is less dogma about varieties, training systems, and vinification approaches, and more willingness to adapt to site-specific conditions.”

Any would-be challenges become opportunities here, leaving winemakers with a lot of runway. Biondi Santi is energized rather than daunted by all the untouched wilderness, because when cultivated properly, the results are staggeringly impressive. He now finds himself in a position where he can watch “innovation and terroir-driven precision coexist,” he said, “allowing producers to define identity through geology and microclimate rather than a historical formula.”

Farther north, in two nearby subregions on the Tuscan coast, Giovanni Frascolla makes wines at his family estates, Tua Rita and Poggio Argentiera. “There is a strong sense of dynamism here and a real drive toward experimentation, partly because we are less constrained by rigid historical regulations,” he said. His wines lean into that flexibility, playing with unexpected varietals like Syrah and Traminer alongside the popular local grapes Sangiovese and Vermentino.

On the other hand, all the coastal subregions of Tuscany do share some common attributes, Frascolla said. “The sea is certainly the element that connects the entire coastal stretch, from Riparbella down to Capalbio, but what is often overlooked is the role of the winds.” The wind keeps things in check, making sure that the sun’s heat never gets excessive, that the humidity doesn’t penetrate the vineyards, and that this generally wild territory doesn’t slowly return to its swampy origins.

His words suggest a fitting analogy to the winds of change as he and his colleagues prepare to sail toward an auspicious new frontier.

Bottles to Look For

Castello di Montepò di Jacopo Biondi Santi 2024 JeT Rosé (Toscana)

Aromas of warm bricks, wild cherries, red plum skin and blood orange create a nose that’s savory, herbaceous and saline, and the palate has a similar feel with more wild cherries and salted slices of blood orange. A wine that’s confident and substantial, but still bracingly fresh. 92 Points — Danielle Callegari

$30 Francos Wine Merchants

Tua Rita 2024 Perlato del Bosco Vermentino (Vermentino di Toscana)

Lemon, lime, grapefruit, fresh cut grass and wild flowers on the nose open up for yellow apple, honey, and nuts on the palate. These notes plus the full mouthfeel make for an incredible richness, but a vein of saline cuts through and citrus lifts up the palate. This is Vermentino living its best life right now. 93 Points — D.C.

$25 The Wine Wave

Fattoria Le Pupille 2024 Sangiovese (Morellino di Scansano)

The nose is a mix of crunchy red fruit and flowers, as cherries mingle with peonies, plus a touch of orange peel for texture and density. Strawberries and raspberries join more cherries on the palate, with earth and bitter citrus underneath. Fresh and vibrant. 91 Points — D.C.

content:
America’s 250th Birthday Calls for a Drink

We may be living through strange, unprecedented times with industry-crippling tariffs, failing wars, and inflation on the rise. But no matter your political affiliation, there’s still plenty to love about the United States of America. Not only are we the birthplace of ranch dressing (we see you, World Cup tourists), but we’re also the originators of wine jelly (yes, you read that right) and the Dirty Spritz. We do wine country road trips like no other, and our winemakers are innovating like never before, with unusual grape varieties, progressive viticulture, and climate-conscious farming. Here’s what we’re toasting to on America’s 250th birthday.

title: Thomas Jefferson Loved This Extremely Weird Wine Jelly
summary:
Made from milk, sugar, and Madeira, this wine jelly is one of the few recipes that can be reliably traced back to America’s third president.

The post Thomas Jefferson Loved This Extremely Weird Wine Jelly appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Want to drink like a Founding Father this Independence Day? Wine jelly is the way to go. This recipe can be traced all the way back to Thomas Jefferson, and was either created by Jefferson himself or by his enslaved chef James Hemming, who learned to cook in France. Made with milk, sugar, lemon juice, and Madeira, the gelatinous concoction is half dessert, half cocktail, and 100% wacky.

“Madeira was the Champagne of the era,” explains Bennett Rea, host and creator of Cookin’ with Congress, a video series where he recreates unhinged recipes from politicians. “It was costlier than any other wine, making it a favorite among presidents and other wealthy Americans.” 

For this recipe, Rea recommends looking for a dry to medium-dry style that you’d enjoy drinking on its own. The fortified wine’s oxidative nuttiness is responsible for elevating the jelly’s flavor profile, so don’t be afraid to splurge on the good stuff.

Did wine jelly inadvertently pave the way for the creation of Jell-O shots? Maybe, but we wouldn’t exactly recommend bringing it to a frat party. Jefferson’s concoction belongs with a scoop of whipped cream, ready to help you properly celebrate America’s 250th birthday.

Thomas Jefferson’s Wine Jelly

Courtesy of Bennett Rea, host and creator of Cookin’ with Congress, adapted from Thomas Jefferson and James Hemming

Ingredients

  • 2 cups milk
  • 1 cup Madeira
  • 3⁄4 cup sugar
  • ½ cup cold water
  • 2 tbsp gelatin
  • 3 lemons
  • Pinch of salt

Instructions

Step 1

Soak 2 tablespoons of gelatin in 1/2 cup cold water until it blooms, about 5 minutes.

Step 2

Bring 2 cups of milk to a boil on low heat, then remove from heat.

Step 3

Add the gelatin to the milk and stir until dissolved.

Step 4

Stir in 3/4 cup of sugar and a pinch of salt. Let cool for 30-40 minutes on the countertop.

Step 5

Add 1 cup of quality madeira and the juice of 3 lemons.

Step 6

Strain through a cheesecloth into a mold.

Step 7

Refrigerate at least 10 hours or overnight.

title: The Great American Road Trip Is Alive and Well in These Wine Regions
summary:
Many of the country’s most esteemed wine-producing regions are not only breathtakingly scenic, but full of charming pit stops that are best accessed by car.

The post The Great American Road Trip Is Alive and Well in These Wine Regions appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
From leisurely drives through mountain passes to legendary coastal routes, there’s no better way to experience America’s best wine regions than with a good-old-fashioned road trip. Many of the country’s most esteemed wine-producing areas, from the Finger Lakes to Walla Walla Valley, are not only breathtakingly scenic, but full of charming towns for pit stops that are best accessed by car. Exploring these regions with a road trip over several days—with ample breaks between vineyard visits to eat, stroll, and even spa—allows you to experience so much more than just the wine. 

Whether you want to enjoy dramatic landscapes between laidback wineries or sip Bordeaux blends at historic inns, the itineraries below provide plenty of inspiration for your next road trip.

The Seneca Lake Loop, New York

If waterfront vistas, trolley rides, and unpretentious tasting rooms sound like your scene, set out on Central New York’s Seneca Lake Wine Trail. Named Wine Enthusiast’s 2025 American Wine Region of the Year, the Finger Lakes are within a five-hour drive of several major metropolises, including New York City and Philadelphia. 

Among the waterways, Seneca Lake has the highest concentration of wineries, many known for their Riesling, Pinot Noir, and ice wines. Brittany Gibson, executive director of the Seneca Lake Wine Trail, recommends family-owned Fox Run Vineyards for an in-depth, hands-on look at the winemaking process. (They offer tours of their winemaking facility seven days a week, all year round.) She also recommends off-the-beaten-path Boundary Breaks Vineyard, which has an on-site dog park and private patios for peaceful lakeside tasting. 

Stop by Keuka Outlet Trail for a leisurely, under-the-radar nature escape with waterfalls and winding streams, as well as nearby Shtayburne Creamery for ice cream and cheese flights. Just before rounding the lake’s northern edge, roam Geneva’s Linden Street, a quaint, walkable neighborhood of historic buildings and wine bars. Cabin and cottage rentals complement Seneca’s homey atmosphere, but for upscale lakeside stays, head to Watkins Glen Harbor Hotel or the romantic Idlwilde Inn.

Read our full insider’s guide to the Finger Lakes from WE Writer-at-Large Christina Pickard.

Santa Barbara to Carmel-by-the-Sea, California

Few roads in America can rival the epic coastal views of Highway 1, which re-opened earlier this year after having been closed since January 2023. A journey from Santa Barbara to Carmel-by-the-Sea, including a lengthy sojourn in Big Sur, lets you experience some of the West’s best wines, luxe hospitality, and dramatic scenery. Start in Santa Barbara’s lively Funk Zone neighborhood to hit the buzziest restaurants and tasting rooms, or if history and relaxation are your top priorities, base yourself in the colonial-era Presidio neighborhood, which boasts Lobero Theatre, the state’s oldest continuously operating theatre since 1873. Centrally located stays at the boutique Riviera Beach House and the Spanish-style Kimpton Canary Hotel (don’t miss the glam rooftop pool) make it easy to explore it all before heading south. 

In Big Sur, stay at the serene, clifftop Post Ranch Inn for the region’s most luxurious accommodations, or enjoy the cozy riverside cabins at Ripplewood Resort. Dine at Wine Enthusiast editor-favorite Solstice to sample a selection of Monterey’s best wine, including Chardonnay and Pinot Noir. End your journey on the cobblestone streets of Carmel-by-the-Sea, with standouts De Tierra and Galante Wines among the storybook town’s best tasting rooms. 

Read WE Writer-at-Large Matt Kettmann’s dreamy guide to eating, drinking, and vibing in Big Sur.

Seattle to Walla Walla Valley, Washington

There are several ways to get from the Emerald City to the world-class Pacific Northwest wine destination, including stunning byways through Yakima River Canyon and Olympic National Park, so take your pick. The best routes by car include stopovers within the historic Yakima Valley AVA and its sub-AVAs, a region where over one-third of Washington’s grapes—and more than 70% of the country’s hops—are grown. 

Picturesque Walla Walla Valley went through a third wave of growth in the early 2020s, with a host of rolling vineyards opening alongside new restaurants and hotels. Morgan Davis, general manager of retro-chic downtown hotel The Finch, recommends starting at Walla Walla Vintners. The vineyard sits at the base of the Blue Mountains, offering stunning alpine views to the east and lavender-tinged sunsets to the west. She also suggests heading to Tranche Estate to enjoy live music with locals and stopping by art-focused Dama Wines downtown, which features the work of a local creative on every label. If you can, snag a reservation at 18-seat Bar Bacetto for James Beard Award-nominated Italian cuisine. 

Get more inspiration from our guide to the best wineries (and more) to visit in Walla Walla Valley.

Exclusive Access to
Wine Country,
Led by Wine Enthusiast

Texas Hill Country

The state’s wine scene continues its meteoric rise, solidifying Texas Hill Country’s reputation as a dream destination for wine lovers. The scenic route between San Antonio and Austin goes through the best of Texas Hill Country, including a dense cluster of wineries along the U.S. Route 290 through Fredericksburg. There are private barrel room tours and tastings at Becker Vineyards, one of the most awarded wineries in the state.The sweeping Hill Country views and celebrated Riesling at Invention Vineyards are also worthy of a visit. 

Nearby, Cabernet Grill serves refined Texas classics, like Gulf shrimp and grilled quail, and an entirely Texan wine list. Book Stonewall Motor Lodge’s apropos accommodations to embrace a nostalgic roadside ambiance, or opt for the fairytale stone cottages at Cotton Gin Village. Then, extend your drive to Austin with a stay on Sage Hill Inn & Spa’s tranquil 88 acres, and be sure to set aside time to enjoy the state capital’s budding wine scene.

We’ve rounded up the best wineries (and more) to visit in Texas right now.

The Monticello Wine Trail, Virginia

Virginia’s viticulture shares a founding father with the nation itself, though the state’s thriving 4,000-plus acres of vineyards likely surpass even Thomas Jefferson’s grandest visions. Setting out on the Monticello Wine Trail, which encompasses 2023 Wine Region of the Year Charlottesville, is a fitting celebration for America’s 250th birthday. According to Richard Leahy, author of Beyond Jefferson’s Vines, the Evolution of Quality Wine in Virginia, Barboursville Winery—the state’s oldest and largest vineyard—is not to be missed. Their signature wine is Octagon, named in tribute to ruins of a home designed by Jefferson on the vineyard’s grounds. Stay at the 1804 Inn and Cottages for full immersion. Leahy also suggests sampling a wide range of Virginia’s best at Horton Vineyards and making a visit to Loving Cup, the state’s only certified organic vineyard. On the eastern side of the trail, you’ll find fantastically scenic roadways and acclaimed vineyards tucked into the bases of Blue Ridge peaks, including King Family Vineyards and Afton Mountain Vineyards.

For the full experience, work your way through the 10 best wineries to visit in Virginia.

Portland to Eugene, Oregon

Willamette Valley’s coveted Pinot Noir earned its reputation as the “Burgundy of the United States,” and winemakers continue to elevate its Chardonnay and sparkling offerings. From Portland, explore the region via Oregon Route 99. Elaine Kim, wine director at The Allison Inn & Spa, says a drive across beautiful Worden Hill Road in Dundee Hills, one of Willamette’s 11 sub-AVAs, is the best way to take it all in. There, Granville Wine Co. combines vast views with a sampling of wines from across the valley, while Soter Vineyards’ farm-to-table pairing menu in the nearby Yamhill-Carlton AVA makes a memorable lunch stop. Travelers should also dedicate a day to the Eola-Amity Hills AVA, where Corollary Winery exclusively serves Oregon sparklers and Brooks Winery crafts some of the region’s only (and best) Riesling. Beyond wine, Willamette has become a star on the national culinary stage: Chef Timothy Westall won a James Beard Award for his work at Antica Terra, and, according to Kim, the hyper-seasonal Hayward Restaurant is also among Willamette’s best, with a menu of Northwestern fare that changes weekly.

Don’t miss the buzzy Willamette Valley restaurants that are putting the area on the culinary map.

title: 6 Reasons to Be Excited About the Future of American Wine
summary:
As the U.S. prepares to celebrate its 250th birthday, our wine reviewers share why the future of American wine looks bright.

The post 6 Reasons to Be Excited About the Future of American Wine appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
With the arrival of the July 4th semiquincentennial, the United States has officially existed for 250 years. American winemaking, on the other hand, has existed much longer. In the late 16th century, French Huguenot settlers made wine with Muscadine varieties near present day Jacksonville, Florida. 

But it wasn’t until 1976 that American wine proved itself on the global stage, when California bottles beat out their French counterparts at the infamous Judgment of Paris tasting, to the shock of the universe. (Jacksonville’s bottles were not tasted, but Florida’s wines have certainly come a long way.)

Despite the current challenges facing the United States’ wine industry, there is so much to be excited about. We asked our writers at large who review wines from U.S. regions—from California to New York and everywhere in between—why the future of American winemaking looks bright.

The New Generation of Finger Lakes Winemakers

“For a wine region to survive and thrive, it needs a balance of longstanding family-run producers and also a cohort of newer arrivals with national and international experience and fresh perspectives. I believe that the Finger Lakes, New York State’s largest wine region, now has that balance right. In recent years, a new generation of talent from both outside and inside the region has helped bring Finger Lakes wines into the future. They’re broadening the scope of drinkers with inclusive messaging and packaging; they’re re-imagining once maligned cold-hardy hybrid varieties, focusing on progressive viticulture like regenerative farming, and thinking outside the winemaking box to create wines of vibrancy, texture and complexity. Not only does this add much-needed color and creativity to an industry sometimes accused of homogeneity, it sets a promising scene for the future of New York State wine. For where the Finger Lakes goes, the rest of the state generally follows.” —Christina Pickard

Unexpected Varietals on the Rise

“This year, I’m excited to see ever more unusual varieties gaining the attention they deserve. Yes, in Napa, Cabernet is king, and in the Russian River Valley, Pinot Noir and Chardonnay are the heavyweights. But growers everywhere are branching out. In Texas, Picpoul Blanc and Tannat are gaining serious traction. In Sonoma, Italian varieties are taking their place at the table with grapes like Vermentino, Grignolino, and Timorasso. Even Pinot Meunier has entered the chat. And have you tried a Napa Valley Scheurebe? Now you can. Don’t be afraid. There’s a world of wine grapes out there, people, so go explore.” —Tom R. Capo

Santa Barbara’s One-Block Challenge 

“It’s really inspiring to watch many of Santa Barbara‘s vintners embrace the One-Block Challenge, which is the Regenerative Viticulture Foundation‘s globe-spanning attempt to turn wine countries onto the benefits and relative ease of regenerative farming. Started just north in Paso Robles but now underway across California, South Africa, Texas, and New Zealand, the simple challenge is to farm one portion of your vineyard—a row, a block, an acre, 10 acres, your call—according to regen principles, and then keep track of how it changes. Santa Barbara’s contingent is especially fired up about this movement, and it gives me a lot of hope for how wine can take the lead on climate issues rather than just be a consumptive industry.” —Matt Kettmann

The (Potential) Comeback of Oregon’s International Pinot Noir Celebration

“The International Pinot Noir Celebration (IPNC) came to an uncertain end in 2024. My days of strolling the Linfield University campus in McMinnville, Oregon, meeting the greatest Pinot Noir fans and producers in the world were over. Recently, however, I learned that the IPNC is entering into a “strategic agreement” with the Willamette Valley Wineries Association. There are no definite plans for an IPNC return as of yet, and the earliest we could see some movement would be in 3 to 4 years. But just the fact that there’s a chance that the greatest three days of wine on earth might return in some form excites me. Maybe Thomas Wolfe was wrong. Maybe you can go home again.” —Michael Alberty

East Coast Viticulture Programs

“While big American enology and viticulture programs, such as UC Davis in California and Cornell University in New York State, have dominated ‘new world’ progression in the wine industry, the acceleration of climate change has aided in fostering smaller, renowned viticultural programs on the East Coast. More localized entities, such as Finger Lakes Community College (New York), Surry Community College (Dobson, North Carolina), the University of Maryland Extension (UME) grape and wine program, and Piedmont Virginia Community College (Charlottesville, VA) are yielding prominent winemakers, agriculturalists, and educators that are adding to the burgeoning growth of the East Coast wine industry. Figures leading the wine surge on the East Coast include, but are not limited to, Dr. Joseph Fiola of Maryland, David Bower and Sarah Bowman of North Carolina, Jake Busching of Virginia, and Paul Brock of the Finger Lakes. Now more than ever, more wine accolades are being awarded to the East Coast regions because of these programs and the fostering of this talent.” —Cheron Cowan

Old Solutions to New Problems

“In my travels across Northern California, I hear stories of faith and resilience as we move forward in our wine community. Lodi is creating new revenue streams through horticulture production and animal husbandry. Livermore is embracing its California wine history; come for the delicious wine, hospitality, and education. Mendocino is going back to its roots, planting, and actively producing ‘otherworldly’ varietals—think Italian grapes, Grüner Veltliner, and Blaufränkisch. Lake County is the perennial location to source volcanic juice for your prized wine project or to produce wine. FYI, you haven’t lived until you drive a 4×4 into the core of a volcano, but more on that next time.”—Tonya Pitts

title: Want to Eat and Drink Like a President? We’re Not So Sure
summary:
On the Wine Enthusiast Podcast, we chat with Bennett Rea, creator of Cookin’ with Congress, about the Founding Fathers’ drinking habits, their weirdest recipes, and how food brings us together.

The post Want to Eat and Drink Like a President? We’re Not So Sure appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
As we approach the 250th anniversary of the United States’ founding, amid such a divisive political climate, it can feel complicated to know exactly how, or what, to celebrate. So this July Fourth, why not take the edge off, and raise a glass to America’s long and winding culinary history?

After all, food is the great unifier, according to Bennett Rea, creator of the popular internet video series Cookin’ With Congress. And he would know, making a living off eating like past presidents, senators, and congresspeople. The results are mixed, to say the least. Rea has tried everything, from William Taft’s lobster stew to Richard Nixon’s favored (…gulp) cottage cheese covered in ketchup to the occasional possum. Recreating and sampling the favorite dishes of these larger-than-life men (and occasionally women) shrinks them back down to human size, and makes them feel a bit more real to the public. We all have to eat, right?

So, as Americans celebrate the nation’s semiquincentennial surrounded by hot dogs and hamburgers, maybe eating like our forefathers is exactly what we need to bring us all together. Aspic of chicken, anyone?

On Odd Presidential Eating Habits

Bennett Rea: “I think the strangest eating habits, for me, are those of people who don’t enjoy food. Calvin Coolidge and Richard Nixon come to mind. Gerald Ford also was quoted as saying, ‘Sleep and food are unnecessary and overrated.’ And I mean, I understood that, because Gerald Ford was drinking a martini with a pile of cottage cheese and raw onion on top, and Richard Nixon was enjoying cottage cheese with ketchup all over it. So, I believe them when they said they did not enjoy food. They didn’t really think about it that much, but it’s so foreign to the way that I appreciate and enjoy life, and I think it is to a lot of others, too. Same with Calvin Coolidge; it’s just kind of like nibbling all day, little snacks just for fuel. That idea of food as fuel, or as a barrier to the rest of your day, is so antithetical to the way I understand life.”

On How Presidents Drank During Prohibition

BR: “Presidents who lived through Prohibition—three out of the four didn’t really play by the rules. They had their sneaky ways of drinking during that time. Calvin Coolidge did play by the rules—maybe that’s, again, why I’m just not relating to him that much, a rule follower and didn’t really enjoy food. Warren G. Harding was president during Prohibition and had sneaky poker games. They would smoke cigars and have whiskey and a lot of beer all night. Hoover, of course, sneaking off to the Belgian Embassy. And then FDR publicly wasn’t drinking, of course, and publicly was not eating or dining on great food during the Great Depression, but privately he was having those cocktails. Although, to be fair, privately, he was eating pretty terrible food as well.”

On FDR, the Mixologist

BR: “After FDR’s meetings were done, at 5:00, 5:30, he would go into a glorified closet in the White House with a few of his closest friends in politics there, five to six of the boys, so to speak. And they would go and have what was called the Children’s Hour. And the Children’s Hour was just FDR whipping up cocktails and drinking and talking and kind of talking trash on all of their political enemies. While Eleanor Roosevelt stayed far away from this, she kind of didn’t really approve of it that much. But he would be mixing up weird versions of martinis, there’s this drink called the Haitian Libation that’s made with rum, orange juice, and brown sugar. And he really fancied himself a home bartender, even if sometimes his contemporaries said that the cocktails tasted like garbage.”

On Taft’s Lobster Stew

BR: “One of my favorites for Taft was this rich lobster stew. It was referenced to and there were some descriptions of it in books that I found, but I really enjoyed making it because I kind of worked on the recipe itself and perfected it over the course of months and months and months… it ended up being years, really. And it’s a gorgeous yellow, it’s popping with tomatoes, it’s got saffron in there. It’s rich but not too rich. I absolutely adore that rich lobster stew.”

On George Washington’s Aversion to Meat

BR: “George Washington, oddly enough, wasn’t a pescatarian, but he did not eat a ton of meat because fish was soft, and he had very bad teeth. And so he would usually prefer fish over other meats. Or, if it was going to be meat, he would have it sliced very, very thin and cooked until it was extremely tender. If he were going to eat tongue, it would be very tender, not tough. But he was a big fish guy. Thomas Jefferson—meats were sort of an accompaniment to vegetables, and [for] John Adams as well. So, really, in the early days of the Founding Fathers, we started off with more alcohol, more vegetables, less meat, and more fish.”

On Food as the Great Humanizer

BR: “I think it humanizes them. I think politicians, and especially certain presidents, have this mythic, legendary status in our minds. They’re like the ‘founding fathers;’ they’re on our money or whatever else. And the series helps bring them down to size just by watching them do—watching me do, I guess—the most ordinary thing that everyone relates to, which is food. And even when it’s weird, I think people connect to that too, because who doesn’t have a strange eating habit? I eat my cereal out of a mug with no spoon. That’s kind of strange, right? Is that any stranger than some of the presidents or politicians of the past? I think everyone’s got those little quirks and habits. And so they can feel seen by Teddy Roosevelt, being like a coffee hound because they’re like, ‘Yo, I drink like 10 to 12 cups a day.’ So I think it’s really fun to see yourself in the presidents.

And even if you don’t, there’s something interesting about the perspective. So, I guarantee 50 years from now, people will look back and be like, ‘You were eating bee pollen? Why did you choke down protein popcorn? What were you doing in that time period?,’ The exact same way that we look at mayonnaise jello today.”

Read more about everything we’re toasting to on America’s 250th birthday.

The Guest

Bennett Rea is the host and creator of Cookin’ with Congress, the viral social media series with over 750,000 followers and 300 million views, where he recreates unhinged recipes from politicians, eats like presidents, and connects to everyday people through the insanity of American food.

More About the Podcast

The Wine Enthusiast Podcast is your serving of drinks culture and the people who drive it. You can subscribe to this podcast on Apple, Spotify, and anywhere else you listen to your favorite shows. Visit the podcast homepage for more episodes and transcripts.

More Podcast Episodes

  • Tyler Balliet transitioned at the peak of her wine career—and wine tastes different now.
  • Everything you need to know about Australia’s most revered cult wine, Wendouree.
  • ‘Don’t drink the faerie wine’ and other important romantasy lessons.
  • Amid a sea of sparkling wines, Moscato d’Asti is the moment.
  • Oregon’s sparkling wines are stunning—does it have what it takes to be the next Champagne?
  • What’s next for the wine importer who took down Trump’s tariffs?
