title: Thomas Jefferson Loved This Extremely Weird Wine Jelly
summary:
Made from milk, sugar, and Madeira, this wine jelly is one of the few recipes that can be reliably traced back to America’s third president.

The post Thomas Jefferson Loved This Extremely Weird Wine Jelly appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Want to drink like a Founding Father this Independence Day? Wine jelly is the way to go. This recipe can be traced all the way back to Thomas Jefferson, and was either created by Jefferson himself or by his enslaved chef James Hemming, who learned to cook in France. Made with milk, sugar, lemon juice, and Madeira, the gelatinous concoction is half dessert, half cocktail, and 100% wacky.

“Madeira was the Champagne of the era,” explains Bennett Rea, host and creator of Cookin’ with Congress, a video series where he recreates unhinged recipes from politicians. “It was costlier than any other wine, making it a favorite among presidents and other wealthy Americans.” 

For this recipe, Rea recommends looking for a dry to medium-dry style that you’d enjoy drinking on its own. The fortified wine’s oxidative nuttiness is responsible for elevating the jelly’s flavor profile, so don’t be afraid to splurge on the good stuff.

Did wine jelly inadvertently pave the way for the creation of Jell-O shots? Maybe, but we wouldn’t exactly recommend bringing it to a frat party. Jefferson’s concoction belongs with a scoop of whipped cream, ready to help you properly celebrate America’s 250th birthday.

Thomas Jefferson’s Wine Jelly

Courtesy of Bennett Rea, host and creator of Cookin’ with Congress, adapted from Thomas Jefferson and James Hemming

Ingredients

  • 2 cups milk
  • 1 cup Madeira
  • 3⁄4 cup sugar
  • ½ cup cold water
  • 2 tbsp gelatin
  • 3 lemons
  • Pinch of salt

Instructions

Step 1

Soak 2 tablespoons of gelatin in 1/2 cup cold water until it blooms, about 5 minutes.

Step 2

Bring 2 cups of milk to a boil on low heat, then remove from heat.

Step 3

Add the gelatin to the milk and stir until dissolved.

Step 4

Stir in 3/4 cup of sugar and a pinch of salt. Let cool for 30-40 minutes on the countertop.

Step 5

Add 1 cup of quality madeira and the juice of 3 lemons.

Step 6

Strain through a cheesecloth into a mold.

Step 7

Refrigerate at least 10 hours or overnight.

title: The Great American Road Trip Is Alive and Well in These Wine Regions
summary:
Many of the country’s most esteemed wine-producing regions are not only breathtakingly scenic, but full of charming pit stops that are best accessed by car.

The post The Great American Road Trip Is Alive and Well in These Wine Regions appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
From leisurely drives through mountain passes to legendary coastal routes, there’s no better way to experience America’s best wine regions than with a good-old-fashioned road trip. Many of the country’s most esteemed wine-producing areas, from the Finger Lakes to Walla Walla Valley, are not only breathtakingly scenic, but full of charming towns for pit stops that are best accessed by car. Exploring these regions with a road trip over several days—with ample breaks between vineyard visits to eat, stroll, and even spa—allows you to experience so much more than just the wine. 

Whether you want to enjoy dramatic landscapes between laidback wineries or sip Bordeaux blends at historic inns, the itineraries below provide plenty of inspiration for your next road trip.

The Seneca Lake Loop, New York

If waterfront vistas, trolley rides, and unpretentious tasting rooms sound like your scene, set out on Central New York’s Seneca Lake Wine Trail. Named Wine Enthusiast’s 2025 American Wine Region of the Year, the Finger Lakes are within a five-hour drive of several major metropolises, including New York City and Philadelphia. 

Among the waterways, Seneca Lake has the highest concentration of wineries, many known for their Riesling, Pinot Noir, and ice wines. Brittany Gibson, executive director of the Seneca Lake Wine Trail, recommends family-owned Fox Run Vineyards for an in-depth, hands-on look at the winemaking process. (They offer tours of their winemaking facility seven days a week, all year round.) She also recommends off-the-beaten-path Boundary Breaks Vineyard, which has an on-site dog park and private patios for peaceful lakeside tasting. 

Stop by Keuka Outlet Trail for a leisurely, under-the-radar nature escape with waterfalls and winding streams, as well as nearby Shtayburne Creamery for ice cream and cheese flights. Just before rounding the lake’s northern edge, roam Geneva’s Linden Street, a quaint, walkable neighborhood of historic buildings and wine bars. Cabin and cottage rentals complement Seneca’s homey atmosphere, but for upscale lakeside stays, head to Watkins Glen Harbor Hotel or the romantic Idlwilde Inn.

Read our full insider’s guide to the Finger Lakes from WE Writer-at-Large Christina Pickard.

Santa Barbara to Carmel-by-the-Sea, California

Few roads in America can rival the epic coastal views of Highway 1, which re-opened earlier this year after having been closed since January 2023. A journey from Santa Barbara to Carmel-by-the-Sea, including a lengthy sojourn in Big Sur, lets you experience some of the West’s best wines, luxe hospitality, and dramatic scenery. Start in Santa Barbara’s lively Funk Zone neighborhood to hit the buzziest restaurants and tasting rooms, or if history and relaxation are your top priorities, base yourself in the colonial-era Presidio neighborhood, which boasts Lobero Theatre, the state’s oldest continuously operating theatre since 1873. Centrally located stays at the boutique Riviera Beach House and the Spanish-style Kimpton Canary Hotel (don’t miss the glam rooftop pool) make it easy to explore it all before heading south. 

In Big Sur, stay at the serene, clifftop Post Ranch Inn for the region’s most luxurious accommodations, or enjoy the cozy riverside cabins at Ripplewood Resort. Dine at Wine Enthusiast editor-favorite Solstice to sample a selection of Monterey’s best wine, including Chardonnay and Pinot Noir. End your journey on the cobblestone streets of Carmel-by-the-Sea, with standouts De Tierra and Galante Wines among the storybook town’s best tasting rooms. 

Read WE Writer-at-Large Matt Kettmann’s dreamy guide to eating, drinking, and vibing in Big Sur.

Seattle to Walla Walla Valley, Washington

There are several ways to get from the Emerald City to the world-class Pacific Northwest wine destination, including stunning byways through Yakima River Canyon and Olympic National Park, so take your pick. The best routes by car include stopovers within the historic Yakima Valley AVA and its sub-AVAs, a region where over one-third of Washington’s grapes—and more than 70% of the country’s hops—are grown. 

Picturesque Walla Walla Valley went through a third wave of growth in the early 2020s, with a host of rolling vineyards opening alongside new restaurants and hotels. Morgan Davis, general manager of retro-chic downtown hotel The Finch, recommends starting at Walla Walla Vintners. The vineyard sits at the base of the Blue Mountains, offering stunning alpine views to the east and lavender-tinged sunsets to the west. She also suggests heading to Tranche Estate to enjoy live music with locals and stopping by art-focused Dama Wines downtown, which features the work of a local creative on every label. If you can, snag a reservation at 18-seat Bar Bacetto for James Beard Award-nominated Italian cuisine. 

Get more inspiration from our guide to the best wineries (and more) to visit in Walla Walla Valley.

Exclusive Access to
Wine Country,
Led by Wine Enthusiast

Texas Hill Country

The state’s wine scene continues its meteoric rise, solidifying Texas Hill Country’s reputation as a dream destination for wine lovers. The scenic route between San Antonio and Austin goes through the best of Texas Hill Country, including a dense cluster of wineries along the U.S. Route 290 through Fredericksburg. There are private barrel room tours and tastings at Becker Vineyards, one of the most awarded wineries in the state.The sweeping Hill Country views and celebrated Riesling at Invention Vineyards are also worthy of a visit. 

Nearby, Cabernet Grill serves refined Texas classics, like Gulf shrimp and grilled quail, and an entirely Texan wine list. Book Stonewall Motor Lodge’s apropos accommodations to embrace a nostalgic roadside ambiance, or opt for the fairytale stone cottages at Cotton Gin Village. Then, extend your drive to Austin with a stay on Sage Hill Inn & Spa’s tranquil 88 acres, and be sure to set aside time to enjoy the state capital’s budding wine scene.

We’ve rounded up the best wineries (and more) to visit in Texas right now.

The Monticello Wine Trail, Virginia

Virginia’s viticulture shares a founding father with the nation itself, though the state’s thriving 4,000-plus acres of vineyards likely surpass even Thomas Jefferson’s grandest visions. Setting out on the Monticello Wine Trail, which encompasses 2023 Wine Region of the Year Charlottesville, is a fitting celebration for America’s 250th birthday. According to Richard Leahy, author of Beyond Jefferson’s Vines, the Evolution of Quality Wine in Virginia, Barboursville Winery—the state’s oldest and largest vineyard—is not to be missed. Their signature wine is Octagon, named in tribute to ruins of a home designed by Jefferson on the vineyard’s grounds. Stay at the 1804 Inn and Cottages for full immersion. Leahy also suggests sampling a wide range of Virginia’s best at Horton Vineyards and making a visit to Loving Cup, the state’s only certified organic vineyard. On the eastern side of the trail, you’ll find fantastically scenic roadways and acclaimed vineyards tucked into the bases of Blue Ridge peaks, including King Family Vineyards and Afton Mountain Vineyards.

For the full experience, work your way through the 10 best wineries to visit in Virginia.

Portland to Eugene, Oregon

Willamette Valley’s coveted Pinot Noir earned its reputation as the “Burgundy of the United States,” and winemakers continue to elevate its Chardonnay and sparkling offerings. From Portland, explore the region via Oregon Route 99. Elaine Kim, wine director at The Allison Inn & Spa, says a drive across beautiful Worden Hill Road in Dundee Hills, one of Willamette’s 11 sub-AVAs, is the best way to take it all in. There, Granville Wine Co. combines vast views with a sampling of wines from across the valley, while Soter Vineyards’ farm-to-table pairing menu in the nearby Yamhill-Carlton AVA makes a memorable lunch stop. Travelers should also dedicate a day to the Eola-Amity Hills AVA, where Corollary Winery exclusively serves Oregon sparklers and Brooks Winery crafts some of the region’s only (and best) Riesling. Beyond wine, Willamette has become a star on the national culinary stage: Chef Timothy Westall won a James Beard Award for his work at Antica Terra, and, according to Kim, the hyper-seasonal Hayward Restaurant is also among Willamette’s best, with a menu of Northwestern fare that changes weekly.

Don’t miss the buzzy Willamette Valley restaurants that are putting the area on the culinary map.

title: 6 Reasons to Be Excited About the Future of American Wine
summary:
As the U.S. prepares to celebrate its 250th birthday, our wine reviewers share why the future of American wine looks bright.

The post 6 Reasons to Be Excited About the Future of American Wine appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
With the arrival of the July 4th semiquincentennial, the United States has officially existed for 250 years. American winemaking, on the other hand, has existed much longer. In the late 16th century, French Huguenot settlers made wine with Muscadine varieties near present day Jacksonville, Florida. 

But it wasn’t until 1976 that American wine proved itself on the global stage, when California bottles beat out their French counterparts at the infamous Judgment of Paris tasting, to the shock of the universe. (Jacksonville’s bottles were not tasted, but Florida’s wines have certainly come a long way.)

Despite the current challenges facing the United States’ wine industry, there is so much to be excited about. We asked our writers at large who review wines from U.S. regions—from California to New York and everywhere in between—why the future of American winemaking looks bright.

The New Generation of Finger Lakes Winemakers

“For a wine region to survive and thrive, it needs a balance of longstanding family-run producers and also a cohort of newer arrivals with national and international experience and fresh perspectives. I believe that the Finger Lakes, New York State’s largest wine region, now has that balance right. In recent years, a new generation of talent from both outside and inside the region has helped bring Finger Lakes wines into the future. They’re broadening the scope of drinkers with inclusive messaging and packaging; they’re re-imagining once maligned cold-hardy hybrid varieties, focusing on progressive viticulture like regenerative farming, and thinking outside the winemaking box to create wines of vibrancy, texture and complexity. Not only does this add much-needed color and creativity to an industry sometimes accused of homogeneity, it sets a promising scene for the future of New York State wine. For where the Finger Lakes goes, the rest of the state generally follows.” —Christina Pickard

Unexpected Varietals on the Rise

“This year, I’m excited to see ever more unusual varieties gaining the attention they deserve. Yes, in Napa, Cabernet is king, and in the Russian River Valley, Pinot Noir and Chardonnay are the heavyweights. But growers everywhere are branching out. In Texas, Picpoul Blanc and Tannat are gaining serious traction. In Sonoma, Italian varieties are taking their place at the table with grapes like Vermentino, Grignolino, and Timorasso. Even Pinot Meunier has entered the chat. And have you tried a Napa Valley Scheurebe? Now you can. Don’t be afraid. There’s a world of wine grapes out there, people, so go explore.” —Tom R. Capo

Santa Barbara’s One-Block Challenge 

“It’s really inspiring to watch many of Santa Barbara‘s vintners embrace the One-Block Challenge, which is the Regenerative Viticulture Foundation‘s globe-spanning attempt to turn wine countries onto the benefits and relative ease of regenerative farming. Started just north in Paso Robles but now underway across California, South Africa, Texas, and New Zealand, the simple challenge is to farm one portion of your vineyard—a row, a block, an acre, 10 acres, your call—according to regen principles, and then keep track of how it changes. Santa Barbara’s contingent is especially fired up about this movement, and it gives me a lot of hope for how wine can take the lead on climate issues rather than just be a consumptive industry.” —Matt Kettmann

The (Potential) Comeback of Oregon’s International Pinot Noir Celebration

“The International Pinot Noir Celebration (IPNC) came to an uncertain end in 2024. My days of strolling the Linfield University campus in McMinnville, Oregon, meeting the greatest Pinot Noir fans and producers in the world were over. Recently, however, I learned that the IPNC is entering into a “strategic agreement” with the Willamette Valley Wineries Association. There are no definite plans for an IPNC return as of yet, and the earliest we could see some movement would be in 3 to 4 years. But just the fact that there’s a chance that the greatest three days of wine on earth might return in some form excites me. Maybe Thomas Wolfe was wrong. Maybe you can go home again.” —Michael Alberty

East Coast Viticulture Programs

“While big American enology and viticulture programs, such as UC Davis in California and Cornell University in New York State, have dominated ‘new world’ progression in the wine industry, the acceleration of climate change has aided in fostering smaller, renowned viticultural programs on the East Coast. More localized entities, such as Finger Lakes Community College (New York), Surry Community College (Dobson, North Carolina), the University of Maryland Extension (UME) grape and wine program, and Piedmont Virginia Community College (Charlottesville, VA) are yielding prominent winemakers, agriculturalists, and educators that are adding to the burgeoning growth of the East Coast wine industry. Figures leading the wine surge on the East Coast include, but are not limited to, Dr. Joseph Fiola of Maryland, David Bower and Sarah Bowman of North Carolina, Jake Busching of Virginia, and Paul Brock of the Finger Lakes. Now more than ever, more wine accolades are being awarded to the East Coast regions because of these programs and the fostering of this talent.” —Cheron Cowan

Old Solutions to New Problems

“In my travels across Northern California, I hear stories of faith and resilience as we move forward in our wine community. Lodi is creating new revenue streams through horticulture production and animal husbandry. Livermore is embracing its California wine history; come for the delicious wine, hospitality, and education. Mendocino is going back to its roots, planting, and actively producing ‘otherworldly’ varietals—think Italian grapes, Grüner Veltliner, and Blaufränkisch. Lake County is the perennial location to source volcanic juice for your prized wine project or to produce wine. FYI, you haven’t lived until you drive a 4×4 into the core of a volcano, but more on that next time.”—Tonya Pitts

title: Want to Eat and Drink Like a President? We’re Not So Sure
summary:
On the Wine Enthusiast Podcast, we chat with Bennett Rea, creator of Cookin’ with Congress, about the Founding Fathers’ drinking habits, their weirdest recipes, and how food brings us together.

The post Want to Eat and Drink Like a President? We’re Not So Sure appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
As we approach the 250th anniversary of the United States’ founding, amid such a divisive political climate, it can feel complicated to know exactly how, or what, to celebrate. So this July Fourth, why not take the edge off, and raise a glass to America’s long and winding culinary history?

After all, food is the great unifier, according to Bennett Rea, creator of the popular internet video series Cookin’ With Congress. And he would know, making a living off eating like past presidents, senators, and congresspeople. The results are mixed, to say the least. Rea has tried everything, from William Taft’s lobster stew to Richard Nixon’s favored (…gulp) cottage cheese covered in ketchup to the occasional possum. Recreating and sampling the favorite dishes of these larger-than-life men (and occasionally women) shrinks them back down to human size, and makes them feel a bit more real to the public. We all have to eat, right?

So, as Americans celebrate the nation’s semiquincentennial surrounded by hot dogs and hamburgers, maybe eating like our forefathers is exactly what we need to bring us all together. Aspic of chicken, anyone?

On Odd Presidential Eating Habits

Bennett Rea: “I think the strangest eating habits, for me, are those of people who don’t enjoy food. Calvin Coolidge and Richard Nixon come to mind. Gerald Ford also was quoted as saying, ‘Sleep and food are unnecessary and overrated.’ And I mean, I understood that, because Gerald Ford was drinking a martini with a pile of cottage cheese and raw onion on top, and Richard Nixon was enjoying cottage cheese with ketchup all over it. So, I believe them when they said they did not enjoy food. They didn’t really think about it that much, but it’s so foreign to the way that I appreciate and enjoy life, and I think it is to a lot of others, too. Same with Calvin Coolidge; it’s just kind of like nibbling all day, little snacks just for fuel. That idea of food as fuel, or as a barrier to the rest of your day, is so antithetical to the way I understand life.”

On How Presidents Drank During Prohibition

BR: “Presidents who lived through Prohibition—three out of the four didn’t really play by the rules. They had their sneaky ways of drinking during that time. Calvin Coolidge did play by the rules—maybe that’s, again, why I’m just not relating to him that much, a rule follower and didn’t really enjoy food. Warren G. Harding was president during Prohibition and had sneaky poker games. They would smoke cigars and have whiskey and a lot of beer all night. Hoover, of course, sneaking off to the Belgian Embassy. And then FDR publicly wasn’t drinking, of course, and publicly was not eating or dining on great food during the Great Depression, but privately he was having those cocktails. Although, to be fair, privately, he was eating pretty terrible food as well.”

On FDR, the Mixologist

BR: “After FDR’s meetings were done, at 5:00, 5:30, he would go into a glorified closet in the White House with a few of his closest friends in politics there, five to six of the boys, so to speak. And they would go and have what was called the Children’s Hour. And the Children’s Hour was just FDR whipping up cocktails and drinking and talking and kind of talking trash on all of their political enemies. While Eleanor Roosevelt stayed far away from this, she kind of didn’t really approve of it that much. But he would be mixing up weird versions of martinis, there’s this drink called the Haitian Libation that’s made with rum, orange juice, and brown sugar. And he really fancied himself a home bartender, even if sometimes his contemporaries said that the cocktails tasted like garbage.”

On Taft’s Lobster Stew

BR: “One of my favorites for Taft was this rich lobster stew. It was referenced to and there were some descriptions of it in books that I found, but I really enjoyed making it because I kind of worked on the recipe itself and perfected it over the course of months and months and months… it ended up being years, really. And it’s a gorgeous yellow, it’s popping with tomatoes, it’s got saffron in there. It’s rich but not too rich. I absolutely adore that rich lobster stew.”

On George Washington’s Aversion to Meat

BR: “George Washington, oddly enough, wasn’t a pescatarian, but he did not eat a ton of meat because fish was soft, and he had very bad teeth. And so he would usually prefer fish over other meats. Or, if it was going to be meat, he would have it sliced very, very thin and cooked until it was extremely tender. If he were going to eat tongue, it would be very tender, not tough. But he was a big fish guy. Thomas Jefferson—meats were sort of an accompaniment to vegetables, and [for] John Adams as well. So, really, in the early days of the Founding Fathers, we started off with more alcohol, more vegetables, less meat, and more fish.”

On Food as the Great Humanizer

BR: “I think it humanizes them. I think politicians, and especially certain presidents, have this mythic, legendary status in our minds. They’re like the ‘founding fathers;’ they’re on our money or whatever else. And the series helps bring them down to size just by watching them do—watching me do, I guess—the most ordinary thing that everyone relates to, which is food. And even when it’s weird, I think people connect to that too, because who doesn’t have a strange eating habit? I eat my cereal out of a mug with no spoon. That’s kind of strange, right? Is that any stranger than some of the presidents or politicians of the past? I think everyone’s got those little quirks and habits. And so they can feel seen by Teddy Roosevelt, being like a coffee hound because they’re like, ‘Yo, I drink like 10 to 12 cups a day.’ So I think it’s really fun to see yourself in the presidents.

And even if you don’t, there’s something interesting about the perspective. So, I guarantee 50 years from now, people will look back and be like, ‘You were eating bee pollen? Why did you choke down protein popcorn? What were you doing in that time period?,’ The exact same way that we look at mayonnaise jello today.”

The Guest

Bennett Rea is the host and creator of Cookin’ with Congress, the viral social media series with over 750,000 followers and 300 million views, where he recreates unhinged recipes from politicians, eats like presidents, and connects to everyday people through the insanity of American food.

I thought it was so funny. I’d always been obsessed with presidents since I was like six years old. I really wanted to be the president of the United States and a chef at the same time. And so I started doing them for my own pleasure back then, one offs here and there started with wine jelly and diabetic cucumber salad and happy ham all from that cookbook. That all sounds extremely fun. Who knows? You might even end up with your own peanut farm, a la Jimmy Carter.

BR

Look, we’ve got chickens. We’ve got three chickens right now. We’ll start there and see how big we can go.

JK

Okay. Bennett, this was amazing. I look forward to the last 10 presidents. Very excited for everything you have coming up and thank you so much for being here.

BR

Oh, thank you so much, Jordon. This was a lovely conversation. I hope tomorrow you wake up and you consider that Harry Truman hard-start to the day. Let me know how it goes.

JK

You know what? You’ll be the first person I reach out to after I try it. Thank you, Bennett.

BR

Thanks.

JK

I hope everyone listening has a better idea of what can be done in the wide world of jello and mushy breads, but mostly I want to thank Bennett for his time and insight. You can check out his content on YouTube, Instagram, and TikTok. You can email us your comments and questions at podcast@wineenthusiast.com. Remember, you can subscribe to this podcast on Apple, Spotify, and anywhere else you listen to your favorite shows. You can also go to wineenthusiast.com/podcast for more episodes and transcripts. I’m Jordon Kaplan. Thanks for listening.

The post Want to Eat and Drink Like a President? We’re Not So Sure appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.

content:
We never say no to a cheeseboard. But when you want to pair your tasting-room visit with a memorable meal, book a table at one of these 57 destination restaurants.

By Nils Bernstein

At their best, winery restaurants do more than just feed tasting-room guests.

They mirror the winery itself: obsessive about place, and determined to say something distinct. That ambition can be surprisingly difficult to pull off, since in much of the world ( especially the U.S.), restrictions around agricultural land and alcohol production make running full-service restaurants at wineries difficult. Furthermore, the winery itself needs to be worth a visit, since you can’t have a great winery restaurant without great wine.

This list celebrates our favorite winery restaurants around the globe, from casual bites overlooking vineyards to elaborate tasting menus that rank among the world’s best. These aren’t just spots for a meat-and-cheese platter between pours. They are destinations to plan trips around and gain a deeper understanding of the wine regions they call home.

Argentina Australia Austria Brazil Canada Chile England France Georgia Germany Greece Hungary Italy Japan Liechtenstein Mexico Moldova New Zealand Portugal South Africa Spain United States Uruguay

Explore by Country

Argentina

Australia

Austria

Brazil

Canada

Chile

Georgia

Germany

Greece

Hungary

Italy

Japan

Liechtenstein

Mexico

Moldova

New Zealand

Portugal

South Africa

Spain

United States

Uruguay

Argentina

Back to Top

ANGÉLICA COCINA MAESTRA

Bodega Catena Zapata

Mendoza, Argentina

Angélica Cocina Maestra turns dining into an exploration of Mendoza’s high-altitude farms and vineyards. Located at Catena Zapata’s pyramid-shaped winery, the restaurant structures tasting menus around specific vineyard elevations and historic vintages. Against the dramatic backdrop of the Andes, Chefs Josefina Diana and Juan Manuel Feijoo rework Argentine traditions, pairing veal sweetbreads with beets, or the slender local pejerrey fish with krein, a horseradish condiment brought by Jewish immigrants.

MARIS RESTÓ

Bodega Roberto Bonfanti

Mendoza, Argentina

For an inland region, Mendoza has exceptional seafood, flown in daily from the coasts. Here, it pairs beautifully with the estate’s wines, a refreshing departure from the area’s usual lineup of beef parrilladas with Malbec and Cabernet. The familial hospitality is what you’d expect from a longstanding family winery, and views of century-old olive trees add to the romance.

RICCITELLI BISTRÓ

Matías Riccitelli Wines

Mendoza, Argentina

Riccitelli Bistró sits among old vines in Las Compuertas, one of Mendoza’s highest and coolest vineyard areas, and the setting shapes much of the experience. Chef Juan Ventureyra cooks nearly everything over an open fire, using produce from the restaurant’s organic garden. The rustic atmosphere fits naturally with Matías Riccitelli’s low-intervention wines and the mountain-framed vineyard landscape.

ROSELL BOHER LODGE

Rosell Boher

Mendoza, Argentina

Long lunches here regularly stretch into the afternoon beside the outdoor pool, usually accompanied by one of Rosell Boher’s traditional-method sparkling wines. Dinner guests get a cozier vibe and an exploration of local and seasonal ingredients, with both mountain and vineyard views.

SAVIA COCINA

Casarena Bodega y Vinedos

Mendoza, Argentina

Mendoza’s slow-paced hospitality is epitomized by the refined-but-not-fussy cuisine at Savia Cocina. Overlooking vineyards in Luján de Cuyo, some of which are nearing a century old, the restaurant centers on open-fire cooking, seasonal produce, and Argentine comfort dishes designed for extended lunches. Mountain views, shaded terraces, and Malbec pairings give the experience an easygoing, celebratory rhythm.

Australia

Back to Top

THE LANE VINEYARD RESTAURANT

The Lane Vineyard

Adelaide Hills, Australia

About 20 minutes from Adelaide, The Lane offers unparalleled views of the Onkaparinga Valley. The menu serves inventive takes on Southern Australian ingredients; a recent meal started with prawn with lemon myrtle, pumpkin with marigold, and turnip with salmon roe. If they have it, order their own vineyard-trodding lamb paired with their famous Syrah.

WILLS DOMAIN

Wills Domain Winery

Margaret River, Australia

Wills Domain has long been one of Margaret River’s standout dining destinations, partly because of its sweeping Gunyulgup Valley views, but mostly for modern Australian menus built around local seafood, produce, and Southwest ingredients. Start with rock oyster with finger lime; end with the winery’s own honey served with native desert lime and quandong fruits, and locally grown mango and ginger.

Austria

Back to Top

SATTLERHOF

Weingut Sattlerhof

Südsteiermark, Austria

Sattlerhof is one of Austria’s leading wine estates, and its Michelin-starred restaurant is set among the rolling hills of the Südsteiermark region, near the Slovenian border. The kitchen highlights seasonal Austrian ingredients prepared with delicacy and precision, like pikeperch with just-shelled peas and venison with foraged morels.

Brazil

Back to Top

ARENITO

Vinícola Uvva

Chapada Diamantina, Brazil

Arenito stands out in part because of its location in Brazil’s Chapada Diamantina, a high-altitude region historically better known for mining than for wine. The restaurant leans into the breadth of Brazilian cuisine, from the traditional (cod with olives and eggs) to the modern (sun-dried local lamb with plantain purée and broccoli foam). The architecture is strikingly contemporary, making the contrast with the rugged surroundings even more dramatic.

Canada

Back to Top

RESTAURANT PEARL MORISSETTE

Pearl Morissette Winery

Niagara Peninsula, Canada

The restaurant at Pearl Morissette Winery has evolved from a modest expression of the region’s farms to one of North America’s most acclaimed and influential restaurants. With a philosophy that both wine and food should, above all, express a sense of place, the leisurely tasting menu focuses on Canadian producers that share this mindset. Namely: a birch and apple custard tart with woodruff ice cream and the coffee-like chaga mushroom that grows on birch trees in eastern Canada’s chilly climate.

OLD VINES RESTAURANT

Quails’ Gate Winery

Okanagan Valley, Canada

Nearly every table here comes with a view of Okanagan Lake, a perfect setting for British Columbian dishes like white king salmon cured with the cylindrical, tart-sweet blue haskap berry or lamb chops with a spruce-tip jus. Dishes are designed to pair with the wines, which include Riesling, Chenin Blanc, Syrah, and Merlot, as well as the fresh and expressive Chardonnays and Pinot Noirs the estate is known for.

Chile

Back to Top

RAYUELA WINE & GRILL

Viu Manent

Colchagua, Chile

Rayuela at Viu Manent captures the more relaxed side of Colchagua wine country. The restaurant is known for clay-oven cooking, Chilean comfort dishes, and outdoor tables surrounded by vines and horse paddocks. Guests often arrive after horseback rides or carriage tours of the estate, which lends the whole place an easygoing atmosphere rooted in the rhythm of the winery itself.

VIK ZERO

VIK Wine

Cachapoal Valley, Chile

VIK Zero feels closer to a contemporary architectural project than a conventional winery restaurant. Located inside VIK’s titanium-roofed winery in Chile’s Cachapoal Valley, the restaurant was designed around sustainability and low-waste cooking. Menus change frequently based on produce from the estate gardens and local suppliers, while panoramic windows keep the surrounding hills and vineyards visible throughout the meal.

England

Back to Top

CHALK

Wiston Estate

Sussex, England

Chalk at Wiston Estate brings a distinctly English approach to wine-country dining. To pair with the sparkling wines they’re known for, you might get über-English dishes like mackerel with horseradish and elderflower, or Devonshire brill with samphire. Local game, meanwhile, might be paired with reds inspired by regions ranging from Australia to Burgundy.

THE RESTAURANT AT HAMBLEDON VINEYARD

Hambledon Vineyard

Hampshire, England

Hambledon is considered England’s oldest commercial vineyard, and its restaurant looks directly over the Hampshire vines that helped revive English sparkling wine. The atmosphere is country-estate, but the menu is proudly modern and seasonal. You may find guinea fowl, lamb sweetbreads, and chalk stream trout on the menu year-round, but paired with asparagus, peas, broccoli sprouts, and wild garlic in spring, and daikon, pickled onion, and chestnut in autumn.

France

Back to Top

CHEZ JEANNETTE

La Commanderie de Peyrassol

Provence, France

Chez Jeannette brings serious culinary ambition to the Provençal countryside, serving seasonal menus built around produce from the estate’s organic garden and nearby farms. Chef Benjamin Le Balch’s cooking remains elegant without losing the relaxed vineyard atmosphere, with dishes like lamb with coffee, beets, and poppy, or sériole (amberjack) with white asparagus and acacia flower vinegar. Long lunches in the glow of southern French light are the way to go.

LA GRAND’VIGNE

Château Smith Haut Lafitte

Pessac-Léognan, France

Located inside the Sources de Caudalie hotel, La Grand’Vigne sits beneath an elegant glass canopy inspired by 18th-century greenhouses, setting the stage for an exacting meal that borders on the theatrical. The two-Michelin-starred restaurant is known for polished French cuisine with a lighter touch; the six-course “Vegetal Terroir” menu is revelatory.

Georgia

Back to Top

CRAZY POMEGRANATE

Pheasant’s Tears

Kakheti, Georgia

Grab some of your favorite natural-wine pals for the custom feast here (reserved for groups of 10 or more), with dozens of Georgian dishes and rare qvevri-aged wines, overlooking vineyards at the base of the Caucasus Mountains. You’ll enjoy chvishtari, khachapuri, mtsvadi, and countless more vegetable-forward dishes, just 10 minutes from Sighnaghi, one of Georgia’s most lovely towns.

Germany

Back to Top

RESTAURANT SCHLOSSSCHÄNKE

Schloss Johannisberg

Rheingau, Germany

Schloss Johannisberg is one of Germany’s most historically significant wine estates, and Restaurant Schlossschänke serves Rheingau cuisine designed to complement the ripeness and sweetness levels of traditional Riesling. Guests dine inside historic castle spaces or on terraces overlooking the Rhine Valley and the dramatically steep Riesling vineyards descending toward the river.

Greece

Back to Top

ANHYDROUS CELLAR DOOR

Anhydrous Winery

Santorini, Greece

There’s nothing cookie-cutter about Anhydrous Winery or its restaurant; both embrace the intense, unique character of this hot, dry, volcanic region, battered by wind and ocean spray. Sweeping volcanic island views pair modern Greek dishes with Assyrtiko and other indigenous varieties, with a setting that feels at once ancient and ultramodern.

Hungary

Back to Top

PADI

Sauska

Tokaj, Hungary

Padi is a contemporary ode to Hungarian wine and Tokaj culinary tradition. Try salt-baked celery with Hungarian sturgeon caviar alongside a brisk Furmint, or Mangalitsa pork neck with cottage cheese dumplings and paprika sauce, paired with a surprisingly fresh and complex Pinot Noir. Save time for a cellar tour and a leisurely glass on the terrace.

BEST RESTAURANT DESIGN: Two intersecting lens-shaped discs, each 120 feet across, hover above a panorama of some of Hungary’s most exalted vineyards. Like a watchman’s eyes, the structure evokes the 24/7 guards who protect Hungary’s famed Holy Crown. Architect Péter Bordás of Hungary’s BORD Architectural Studio sought not to integrate the structure with nature but to stand in reverence of it. “What I create should not touch the untouchable,” he says. Built as much to accommodate Padi restaurant and bar as the winery itself, it’s a sublime ode to the great wine, hospitality, and dry humor that epitomize modern Hungary.

Italy

Back to Top

IL RISTORO DI CASTELLO DI AMA

Castello di Ama

Chianti, Italy

Housed in an 18th-century villa, Il Ristoro di Castello di Ama feels less like a standalone restaurant and more like an extension of Castello di Ama itself. Opt for the “Symposium” or “Feast” menus, which include intimate tours of the property, cellar, and the contemporary art installations scattered throughout. The kitchen focuses on traditional Tuscan cooking with a refined touch, while the quiet Chianti setting gives the experience an unhurried, almost private atmosphere.

INDIGENO

Salcheto Winery

Montepulciano, Italy

Indigeno reflects Salcheto’s longstanding focus on sustainable viticulture and low-impact production. The restaurant centers on seasonal ingredients that are regularly paired with Salcheto’s exceptional Sangioveses, produced at one of Italy’s best-known sustainable estates. The menu is closely connected to the surrounding landscapes, with salads of foraged greens, local game and deer, and breads and pastas made daily.

LA VEDUTA

Tenuta Cavalier Pepe

Campania, Italy

La Veduta makes the most of its position above the hills of Irpinia in Campania, focusing on regional dishes paired with estate wines made from Fiano, Greco, and Aglianico. Handmade pasta, slow-cooked meats, and produce from the surrounding countryside give the menu a distinctly local identity despite the polished winery setting.

LOCANDA COSTASERA AT MONTELEONE21

Masi Agricola

Valpolicella, Italy

There’s a “wine first” philosophy here, meaning that food is paired with the wine, not the other way around. Try tubetti pasta with peas, sumac, Roman mint, and goat cheese with a light-bodied natural Corvina-Merlot blend that provides a glittering backdrop of tart red fruit. The restaurant channels the warm, traditional side of Valpolicella hospitality, with stone walls, rustic interiors, and vineyard surroundings.

OSTERIA DI BROLIO

Barone Ricasoli

Chianti, Italy

Osteria di Brolio sits directly below Brolio Castle, surrounded by vineyards tied to some of Chianti Classico’s longest winemaking traditions. The menu combines Central Italian staples with a modern edge, like pappardelle al ragù, but with a duck ragù, wild fennel, and local olives. Meals tend to unfold slowly on the terrace, overlooking the hills around Gaiole.

RISTORANTE CAMPO DEL DRAGO

Castiglion del Bosco

Montalcino, Italy

Part of the expansive Castiglion del Bosco estate in Montalcino, where Brunello production and luxury hospitality are deeply intertwined, Ristorante Campo del Drago combines vineyard views with elegant Michelin-starred dining. While Brunello country usually sits somewhere between rustic charm and quiet luxury, Campo del Drago (and the broader atmosphere across the property) makes the case for the region as a luxe dining destination.

Japan

Back to Top

APERÇU

Niki Hills Winery

Hokkaido, Japan

Located in Hokkaido’s emerging wine region, your meal begins with a nature walk through the property’s fruit orchards and forests. The menu blends French technique with Hokkaido ingredients, like a papery rice wafer filled with foie gras and sweet bean paste, paired with a sparkling rosé made from Kerner, Bacchus, Cabernet Mitos, and Cabernet Dorsa grapes.

Liechtenstein

Back to Top

TORKEL

Liechtenstein Winery

Rheintal, Liechtenstein

Torkel combines fine dining with a sense of Liechtenstein history that few winery restaurants can match. The restaurant sits inside a former medieval wine building in the princely Herawingert vineyard north of Vaduz, owned by the Liechtenstein family since 1712. The menu moves between refined dishes like kingfish with grapefruit and bison filet, while guests drink wines produced from the vines directly outside the dining room.

Mexico

Back to Top

BÁRBARO ASADOR DE CAMP

Vinos Barrigones

Querétaro, Mexico

Bárbaro is an open-air, open-fire restaurant set among estate vineyards and a natural winery, where you can see the workers in action from your table. It’s worth the day trip from Mexico City, but you may end up staying longer for dishes like a taco of local Wagyu cross on an heirloom corn tortilla, crusted with griddled Oaxacan cheese and topped with avocado, chimichurri, and charred green chilies, and whole-fish “chicharrón,” torn into crispy chunks to dunk in nước chấm and tartar sauce.

LUNARIO

Vinícola La Lomita

Valle de Guadalupe, Mexico

Even in Valle de Guadalupe, a wine region well known for its culinary offerings, Lunario stands apart. Helmed by Chef Sheyla Alvarado, the tasting menu changes monthly and sources produce from the gardens at nearby sister winery and farm Finca La Carrodilla, and seafood like oysters, clams, abalone, sea urchin, and lobster from waters just 10 miles away. Delicate and painterly but never fussy, you may find oysters paired with kumquat, or smoked shrimp with bougainvillea.

Moldova

Back to Top

BUFNIȚA ALBĂ

Castel Mimi Winery

Bulboaca, Moldova

Bufnița Albă is modern Moldova at its best. The restaurant is part of one of the country’s best-known wine estates, originally established in 1893. Menus draw heavily on Moldovan culinary traditions, and the majestic castle architecture and formal landscaping lend a theatrical feel to the property.

New Zealand

Back to Top

AMISFIELD

Amisfield Winery

Central Otago, New Zealand

The setting is lovely—views of vineyards, Lake Hayes, and the Remarkables mountain range—but all eyes are on the plate. Amisfield is as much about terroir as it is about the winery. The tasting menu can reach 20 dishes, each listed by its central ingredients, most of which are native to New Zealand, such as wild pūtangitangi, tītī, mārari, and tāwaka. A bucket-list experience.

CRAGGY RANGE

Craggy Range Winery

Hawke’s Bay, New Zealand

Craggy Range offers one of New Zealand’s most imposing backdrops, with the jagged peak of Te Mata visible beyond the vineyards. The menu centers around ingredients from the estate’s organic culinary garden, with seafood featured prominently. A kumara (sweet potato) pancake comes with crayfish tartare, and pāua (abalone) is served with crumbed green-lipped mussels and Champagne sauce.

Portugal

Back to Top

BOMFIM 1896

Quinta do Bomfim

Douro, Portugal

Most restaurants say they’re inspired by old family traditions and classic hospitality, but few walk the walk like this restaurant from the iconic Port producer Symington. Set on the riverbanks in Pinhão, the restaurant centers its approach on wood fires, open kitchens, and recipes inspired by Douro home cooking, such as smoked eel and slow-roasted baby goat.

MALHADINHA NOVA

Herdade da Malhadinha Nova

Alentejo, Portugal

Malhadinha Nova feels less like a winery visit and more like staying at a stylish estate in the Alentejo region. The restaurant draws heavily from ingredients raised or grown on the property, including the coveted black Iberian pigs. Traditional Alentejo cooking appears throughout the menu, but is presented with a lighter, more contemporary touch that matches the estate’s fresh, modern wines and atmosphere of understated luxury.

MESA DE LEMOS

Quinta de Lemos

Dão, Portugal

The architecture of this restaurant follows the terrain of its hilltop location, with floor-to-ceiling glass that gives an alien vibe even as it seems to have grown out of the granite soil. Chef Diogo Rocha’s tasting menus epitomize modern Portuguese cooking, with each course focused on a single ingredient, like pandora fish from the Algarve, baby goat from Serra do Caramulo, or pineapple from the Azores.

South Africa

Back to Top

BEYOND

Buitenverwachting Wine Farm

Constantia, South Africa

Beyond at Buitenverwachting brings a modern edge to one of Constantia’s oldest wine estates. Chef Peter Tempelhoff offers a personal take on seasonal South African ingredients in his tasting menus, while the dining room overlooks the slopes of the Silvermine Mountain Range. The contrast between the historic Cape Dutch setting and the contemporary cooking creates a balance of tradition and innovation.

DELAIRE GRAFF RESTAURANT

Delaire Graff Estate

Stellenbosch, South Africa

Delaire Graff Estate leans unapologetically into luxury, but the setting draws much of the attention. Perched on Helshoogte Pass above Stellenbosch, the winery pairs vineyard views with South African art, sculpture, and dramatic architecture. The restaurant focuses on ingredients from the estate gardens and regional producers, morphed into artful dishes like raw springbok with fuzzy kei apple, or wagyu tongue with slaphakskeentjies (a sweet-and-sour onion dish), amasi (a fermented milk product), samphire, and rhubarb.

RUST EN VREDE RESTAURANT

Rust en Vrede

Stellenbosch, South Africa

Inspired by the owner’s visit to The French Laundry, Rust en Vrede has exemplified fine dining in Stellenbosch for nearly 20 years. The restaurant is housed in a 1780 Cape Dutch building that was once the historic estate’s cellar. Italian-Brazilian Chef Fabio Daniel brings global influences to the restaurant’s core concept of modern French cuisine, preparing rock lobster with coconut and apricot, for instance. Choose the “Experience” tasting menu, which is custom-designed for the diner and paired with the estate’s rarest bottles.

Spain

Back to Top

REFECTORIO

Abadía Retuerta

Ribera del Duero, Spain

Refectorio occupies a former 12th-century abbey dining hall, and few winery restaurants can match its sense of place. The Michelin-starred restaurant pairs Ribera del Duero wines with highly detailed tasting menus built around refined, almost austere, takes on traditional dishes that can feel practically medieval: pickled partridge, for example, or baby lamb in an eggplant stew called alboronía. Vaulted ceilings, candlelight, and monastery stone walls create a dramatic backdrop.

RESTAURANTE AMBIVIUM

Pago de Carraovejas

Ribera del Duero, Spain

Ambivium at Pago de Carraovejas was designed around the relationship between wine and food, not the other way around. The Michelin-starred restaurant offers tasting menus in which every course is connected to a specific wine pairing from the Pago de Carraovejas cellar. Dining rooms overlook the vineyards of Ribera del Duero, and the experience moves at a pensive pace that encourages guests to pay attention to every glass.

United States

Back to Top

AMBAR ESTATE

Ambar Estate Winery

Newberg, Oregon

The five-course tasting menu of local and organic ingredients—such as stinging nettle dumplings in clarified rhubarb broth—shares this Willamette Valley winery’s farming philosophies. Offered in concert with the winery visit, it nonetheless reads more as a restaurant than another wine-tasting experience. Don’t miss the burrata, paired with whatever fruit and herbs are in season, plus seasonings like crispy shallots, togarashi, and saba, made by reducing the estate’s grape must to a syrup.

THE BISCUIT

Los Milics Vineyards

Elgin, Arizona

This restaurant sits at the base of the Mustang Mountains in Arizona wine country and is named for the distinctive peak locals call “The Biscuit.” The architecture is striking, and the patio overlooks the high-desert grasslands of the Sonoita AVA. Chef Trevor Routman’s cooking keeps the experience grounded in the region, with dry-aged steak from Vera Earl Ranch, 15 minutes away, served with grilled oyster mushrooms and blackberry vinaigrette, paired with a Petit Verdot that exudes smoky dark-chocolate and foraged-berries notes.

BUBBLE ROOM

J Vineyards & Winery

Healdsburg, California

The titular “bubble” here refers as much to the custom cloud chandeliers as J’s acclaimed sparkling wines, since you’ll get plenty of still white and red wine with the five-course tasting menu. A recent menu included a local giant rock scallop with cherry salsa macha, apricot soubise, and the briny vegetable agretti, paired with a juicy Russian River Valley Estate Pinot Gris that has complementary notes of lemongrass and ginger.

CHATEAU MORRISETTE RESTAURANT

Chateau Morrisette

Floyd, Virginia

Chateau Morrisette Restaurant sits high along the Blue Ridge Parkway, where mountain views compete with the food for attention. Their farm-based southern Appalachian and French cuisine features ingredients sourced mostly from their own gardens and regional farms. Fireside dining in cooler months and terrace seating in warmer weather make it one of Virginia wine country’s most scenic year-round destinations.

CHEF’S COUNTER AT BURNT HILL FARM

Burnt Hill Farm

Clarksburg, Maryland

Not just one of the best winery restaurants but also one of the best new(ish) restaurants in the country, Chef’s Counter at Burnt Hill Farm is an intimate tasting counter seating just a handful of guests, with menus shaped by Chef Tae Strain’s Korean-influenced cooking. Ingredients come from the property’s 117-acre regenerative farm, and the experience is enlivened by Burnt Hill’s low-intervention wines.

NINER WINE ESTATES RESTAURANT

Niner Wine Estates

Paso Robles, California

The hospitality at Niner starts on “Heart Hill,” where century-old trees are planted in a heart shape. The food is equally romantic, sourced from a chef’s garden with over 300 edible crops in addition to their own honey and eggs; look for dishes seasoned with lavender, lemon verbena, and magnolia petals. While vegetable heavy, the menu often features unusual cuts like rib eye spinalis and pork secreto.

PALLADIO RESTAURANT

Barboursville Vineyards

Barboursville, Virginia

Palladio reflects the Italian roots of Barboursville Vineyards, which is known for Italian varieties like Sangiovese, Barbera, and Nebbiolo. But like the best Italian cuisine, it’s driven less by repetition and more by ingredients, many of which are grown on site. It results in inventive but no less comforting dishes like risotto of baby octopus and Malabar spinach, and meats like bison and elk.

SORRENTO’S RISTORANTE

Tsillan Cellars

Chelan, Washington

People tend to want vineyard views at a winery restaurant, but here all eyes are on gorgeous Lake Chelan, one of the most romantic wine regions and vacation areas in Washington State. Sorrento’s brings a little Italy to the area, with Tuscan columns on the building and dishes featuring the ingredient-focused cucina povera of Tuscany, using local meat and produce.

STOUT’S SIGNATURE AT GRAPE CREEK

Grape Creek Vineyards

Fredericksburg, Texas

The restaurant sits beside the vineyards at Grape Creek Vineyards along Highway 290, serving dishes like tenderloin bolognese and signature meatballs alongside estate wines. It also boasts the region’s best Sunday brunch, with tables laden with dishes like Bourbon shrimp and grits with homemade andouille sausage and a breakfast burger with egg, bacon, and avocado. A mini pancake stack for the table is a must. Pair it with their Cabernet Trois, a bold blend of Cab Sauvignon, Cab Franc, and Ruby Cabernet.

TABOR HILL RESTAURANT

Tabor Hill Winery

Buchanan, Michigan

Tabor Hill has been a southwest Michigan wine country institution for more than 50 years. Try the local walleye with Dijon cream and seasonal vegetables, or grilled chicken thighs over Asiago gnocchi napped with both white-wine velouté and basil-pistachio pesto. A dozen wines—dry and semi-sweet and fruit wines—pair remarkably well with the hearty fare.

THE KITCHEN AT ABEJA

Abeja Winery

Walla Walla, Washington

The restaurant sits on Abeja’s 49-acre farmstead, a short drive out of downtown Walla Walla, surrounded by vineyards, gardens, and wide open views. Executive Chef (and multiple James Beard Award nominee) Mike Easton’s Tuscan heart meets local ingredients like cherries, apples, pears, asparagus, sunchokes, corn, and an array of exotic potatoes.

THE RESTAURANT AT DELILLE CELLARS

DeLille Cellars

Woodinville, Washington

A short drive from Seattle, Woodinville is a hub of Washington wine, though much of it is served at warehouses where the wines, thankfully, outshine the industrial atmosphere. It’s why a meal at DeLille is so welcome, with sweeping vineyard views and food that screams “Western Washington,” like dandelion risotto with sunflower pistou and rhubarb, or Dungeness crab and corn pappardelle (order the double portion of crab).

VERAISONS RESTAURANT

Glenora Wine Cellars

Dundee, New York

Overlooking Seneca Lake in the heart of Finger Lakes wine country, Veraisons works closely with local farmers and changes its menus seasonally to highlight regional ingredients. The menu curiously combines Italian and pan-Asian ingredients in a way that somehow works brilliantly, like lemongrass chicken pot stickers followed by seafood garganelli with gremolata. Cathedral ceilings and a stone fireplace create a cozy atmosphere, while the terrace opens directly onto vineyard and lake views.

Uruguay

Back to Top

BODEGA GARZÓN RESTAURANT

Bodega Garzón

Maldonado, Uruguay

With Argentinian fire master Francis Mallmann as culinary director, wood-fire cooking rules at this obligatory stop in Uruguay wine country. Cocina rioplatense is the term for food that straddles both countries, but besides the fire-grilled meats that characterize their asados, Garzón’s tasting menus also feature fish and shrimp from Uruguay’s gorgeous coasts. The seemingly endless view passes over rocky vineyards to the ocean beyond.

RESTAURANT LAS ESPINAS

Bodega Bouza

Canelones, Uruguay

A short drive through Chardonnay, Tannat, Pinot Noir, and Merlot vineyards leads to this hilltop restaurant with a 360-degree view that overlooks vines on one side and the Atlantic Ocean on the other. The setting, combined with Uruguayan grilled beef and lamb, housemade cheeses and charcuterie, and a smattering of Spanish dishes like pulpo a la gallega and croquetas de jamón, makes it a restaurant where one can spend the whole day.

Letter from the Publisher

Italy, From the Mouths of Babes

Italian history captured my children’s attention faster than any gelato.

We recently took our five- and eight-year-old boys on their first trip to Italy, and it felt both like a privilege and a small act of parenting bravery.

Read More

My husband and I have always been ambitious, perhaps delusional, about traveling with our children. We have largely white-knuckled our way through international trips, forgoing sleep schedules, expensive childcare, and iPads (except on flights, when kids and adults are allowed unlimited screen time). Sure, my five-year-old colored all over the white comforter in our hotel room because he thought it would “look cool,” but everything mostly worked out fine.

When you picture kids in Italy, you probably imagine them ravenously eating pizza and gelato on ancient streets. And while there was plenty of that, we also taught them how to make pici, a hand-rolled Tuscan pasta I have only ever encountered in the region. They consumed so much prosciutto that they were buoyant with salt during their nightly bath.

But surprisingly, food was not their favorite part of the trip. What captured their attention more than the gelato was the history. Their minds were repeatedly blown by stories of Ancient Rome, gladiators, and just how old everything is. As American-born children (myself included, frankly), it is hard to grasp just how young the United States is in the timeline of global history. The scale of Europe bends your understanding of time in a way that feels almost impossible to explain until you witness it through a child’s eyes.

And that is the wonder of traveling with children: They notice what adults have long stopped seeing.

In Tuscany, we stayed at an agriturismo near Montepulciano, waking to rolling hills so beautiful they almost looked fake. But one of the most memorable moments of the trip was visiting Bindi Sergardi in Siena and spending time with its proprietor, Alessandra Casini Bindi Sergardi, and her sister, Maddalena. To explain Alessandra’s place in Siena’s history to my sons, I referred to her as the “Queen of Tuscany.” Disney lore is a useful shortcut when explaining aristocratic lineage to children. She’s the 23rd-generation steward of her family’s estate, so saying she’s in the “royal family of Siena” isn’t even much of an exaggeration.

Upon entering her home, my boys were fascinated by the ancient Etruscan architecture surrounding them. We kept trying to explain what “nearly 3,000 years old” actually means, and that these stones existed long before the United States, before Pokémon, and even before Ancient Rome and the gladiators. You could almost see their brains short-circuiting. And isn’t that what brings so many of us back to Italy again and again? The well of wonder never runs dry.

For this issue, we found ourselves once again captivated by Italy’s seemingly endless stories of history, food, and wine. There is always something left untold, another bottle to discover, another road not taken, another layer of history beneath your feet.

My boys reminded me of something I had begun taking for granted: Italy’s capacity for storytelling knows no bounds.

title: This Summery Matcha Gin Cocktail Comes from One of Brooklyn’s Coolest Rooftops
summary:
The Evergreen cocktail from Brooklyn’s Bar Blondeau is refreshing without being overly sweet and layered without feeling complicated.

The post This Summery Matcha Gin Cocktail Comes from One of Brooklyn’s Coolest Rooftops appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Jarett Karlsberg wanted to be an actor, but found his stage at Brooklyn’s Wythe Hotel in Williamsburg as beverage director for both Le Crocodile and Bar Blondeau, the French-inspired rooftop cocktail bar. At first glance, the transition from theater to hospitality might seem unexpected, but Karlsberg sees little difference between the two.

“I love making people feel happy, giving them new experiences they can take with them,” he says. “It’s really thrilling to introduce people to new spirits.” That philosophy shapes the drink menu at Bar Blondeau, where he and his team collaborate twice a year to build cocktails around seasonal ingredients and New York producers.

When it comes to designing a drink, Karlsberg compares the process to an actor learning their lines. “If you don’t know your specs, you’re not going to put on a good show,” he says. 

Take the Evergreen, an original cocktail created for Bar Blondeau’s seasonal menu that showcases the bar’s ingredient-first approach to drink making. Rather than relying on infusions, fat-washing, or other modern techniques, Karlsberg builds cocktails around thoughtfully selected spirits, allowing each ingredient to contribute its own distinct role.

“It’s very important to us that every cocktail has a beginning, middle, and end,” Karlsberg says. “You should be able to taste all the elements in some way.” The result is a drink that doesn’t fit neatly into any one cocktail category, but instead reveals itself in layers—from cooling botanicals and grassy matcha to bright citrus and a gently bittersweet finish.

A New Summer Cocktail Classic

The Evergreen cocktail began as an ode to a former colleague of the beverage team who passed away last summer. She was obsessed with matcha and always pushed the team to drink it, but Karlsberg wasn’t convinced it would make for a good cocktail. It was essential to create something that wouldn’t separate, would be shelf-stable, and delivered on the balance his team strives for. It took weeks to perfect, but its success has Karlsberg thinking the Evergreen will play a recurring role on the summer menu.  

As it should. At first sip, it tastes like a cucumber smoothie—something you’d seek post workout. As someone who doesn’t drink matcha, I’m incredibly surprised by how smooth and creamy it is, while also being both vegetal and fruit-forward without one note overpowering the other. It tastes deceptively healthy and therefore perfect for unwinding on a hot summer day. 

The grassy richness of ceremonial-grade matcha with cooling cucumber and watermelon gin, bright lemon, and delicate jasmine creates a freshness and vibrancy. Hints of strawberry and caramelized sweetness add depth, while a gentle bitter finish keeps every sip refreshing and balanced. Served over crushed ice and finished with a cucumber spear, the drink is refreshing without being overly sweet and layered without feeling complicated.

The Ingredients, As Explained by Karlsberg

Fort Hamilton New World Cucumber Watermelon Gin: “We chose Fort Hamilton New World Gin for its refreshing botanicals—cannonball watermelon and cucumber—which play wonderfully with the matcha.” 

Yoshi Matcha Liqueur: “We were looking for something made with real ceremonial-grade matcha that was delicious, wouldn’t separate too quickly in the cocktail, was shelf-stable, and wasn’t too polarizing. Yoshi ticked all those boxes.”

Matchbook Distilling Daytrip Strawberry Amaro: “Matchbook Distilling is an incredible local distillery that makes a wide range of truly impressive and unique spirits and liqueurs. Their Strawberry Amaro pairs beautifully with anything that has a slightly creamy texture (like matcha), and the touch of strawberry lifts all the other flavors.”

Muyu Jasmine Vert: “Made from yuzu, neroli, jasmine, and patchouli, a dash of Muyu Jasmine Vert adds floral and green aromatic notes to the cocktail.”

Cane Syrup: “Provides a touch of caramelized sweetness.”

Lemon Juice: “Fresh lemon juice provides the acidity needed to balance the other ingredients.”

The Evergreen Cocktail

Yield: 1

Ingredients

  • 1½ ounces Fort Hamilton New World Cucumber Watermelon Gin
  • ¾ ounce Yoshi Matcha Liqueur
  • ¼ ounce Matchbook Distilling Daytrip Strawberry Amaro
  • ¾ ounce fresh lemon juice
  • ¼ ounce cane syrup

Instructions

  1. Add all ingredients to a cocktail shaker with ice.
  2. Shake vigorously.
  3. Strain over crushed or pebble ice.
  4. Garnish with a cucumber spear.

Can You Substitute the Ingredients?

The Evergreen is an original recipe, but a few substitutions work well.

  • No cane syrup? Use simple syrup or honey syrup
  • Can’t find Fort Hamilton New World Gin? A cucumber-forward gin, such as Hendrick’s, makes a good substitute, though you’ll lose some of the watermelon character.
  • No Daytrip Strawberry Amaro? Dolin Chambéryzette or strawberry syrup can work, though both produce a sweeter cocktail.

What to Serve With It

Despite its health-food appearance, the Evergreen pairs with a surprisingly wide range of dishes. Karlsberg especially recommends:

  • Curry dishes
  • Spicy cuisine
  • Barbecue
  • Grilled meats
  • Roasted meats
  • Cheese boards

More Cocktail Coverage

  • Stir some sherry into the classic Paloma for an effortlessly delicious, low-proof cocktail.
  • This just in: Protein has come for cocktails.
  • Want to drink like a movie star? Shake up the official Oscars afterparty cocktails.
  • We’re begging you: Stop asking ChatGPT for drink recommendations.

title: This Italian Wine Paradise Is More Than Just a Pretty Coastline
summary:
Yes, Sardinia's beaches are stunning, but the best parts of the island lie off the beaten path.

The post This Italian Wine Paradise Is More Than Just a Pretty Coastline appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
In Italy, when warm weather rolls around, and everyone starts talking about their vacation plans, you will inevitably hear a million Italians repeat the same phrase about their two most famous islands: “If you want culture, go to Sicily; if you want the beach, go to Sardinia.”

So when I was 21, studying in Florence, and had two invitations for summer break—one to Sicily and one to Sardinia—it was a no-brainer. I spent three weeks with my friends in Santa Teresa Gallura, at the northernmost point of Sardinia, bouncing around the spectacular seaside, drinking way too much Ichnusa (the local beer) and mirto (the local digestif), and being generally cultureless. Though I’ve never managed to secure that much time off since, I often return to the island to enjoy its exceptional beaches whenever I need a break.

That said, in the intervening years I’ve developed slightly more sophisticated tastes, and it’s occurred to me that Sardinia has been unfairly characterized as a place that’s beautiful but without substance. Anyone who bothers to comb past its sandy shores will find layers of profundity rarely seen elsewhere. It is, however, a challenging island to get to know, in all senses. Home to flora and fauna not found elsewhere in the Mediterranean and dotted with thousands of megalithic structures that do not resemble the dwellings of any other ancient civilization, the island is so totally alien that some serious academics have even proposed it was the real-life place Plato described when he wrote about Atlantis.

Populated by a people as unique as their island and filled with what can be described without exaggeration as some of the greatest wonders of the prehistoric world, Sardinia is, most alluringly, home to rich, singular food and wine traditions, which is what brings me here this time. I’m hoping to figure out how this magnificent place, filled with ancient secrets and infinite enogastronomic delights, has somehow stayed off everyone’s radar until now. To get my feet wet, I’m staying in a guest house at one of the largest contiguous vineyards in all of Europe: Sella&Mosca’s 1600-acre estate outside of the city of Alghero. Yet even with all the vines surrounding me and a chaise longue on the sun-drenched terrace begging to be paired with a cold glass of bubbles, all I can think about is the depth of history I sense in this place.

Clocking my thirst for a cultural excursion, Sella&Mosca’s hospitality manager and brilliant historian of Sardinia, Anna Cadeddu, suggests we pair their salty, structured Vermentino and Cannonau wines with some historical highlights. This is how I end up inside the domus de janas (Sardinian for “house of fairies” or “witches”), the approximately 5,000-year-old chamber tombs at the Necropolis of Anghelu Ruju, an archaeological site on the Sella&Mosca grounds. We also tour nuraghe, prehistoric structures with gravity-defying stone towers that have somehow stayed in place for millennia. Along the way, we see the remaining devastation of 18th-century Savoyard colonialist deforestation, the architectural reconfigurations that allowed the island to receive electricity at the beginning of the 20th century, and the internment camps where prisoners of war were kept during World War I and II.

We stop at the coastline, prompting Anna to ask, “You do know the story of what happened with Sardinian land toward the end of the last century?” Women had always been bequeathed the ostensibly valueless parcels near the coast, while men enjoyed the family’s arable and grazable land and all the profits it generated. In the 1990s, when developers scrambled to find more space for luxury beachfront hotels on the north side of the island, women found themselves in possession of some of the most desirable land in the world. It’s an especially Sardinian story. With patience, people realized that something that seemed only superficially attractive reveals itself to be, in fact, precious. All the fruits born of the interior—wine grapes included—could only be appreciated once there was a stage where they could be shared with the world: the coastline.

Deeper inland, in the Mandrolisai region, the landscape becomes so lush it’s almost jungly. While the land here is covered in vines and nearly everyone is involved in the wine business, the area is little known for its wine outside Sardinia, not least because it produces very little. People in Mandrolisai refer to wineries that make 40,000 bottles a year as “huge” and are indifferent toward vineyards that are “very young,” which is to say anything less than 50 years old. Anyone who finds their wines will be richly rewarded, because winemakers here take the prehistoric depth trapped in the slivers of land and convert the terroir of this barely penetrable region into wines that seethe with energy and potential.

I meet Iuri Marras from Vigne Centro Sardegna winery. Iuri asks if I want to meet his grandfather Antonio, who taught him how to make wine and who, he promises, is quite a character. We find him on a hill at the top of his garden. Iuri beckons him to come meet me, as I’ve come all the way here from America. His grandfather calls down, “I’ve never been to America, and now I’m never going because of Trump!” It’s delightfully unexpected that this apparently isolated farmer in the Sardinian hinterland is following American politics—even if it only encourages him to feel more distant from the country. Though if he did want to visit, it’s easier now than ever: The island recently got its first nonstop flight to New York City.

As we walk through his garden, Antonio holds back branches for me to pass and picks fresh strawberries for me to eat. He says that winemaking has always been hard here, and now he’s too tired for it, so he just tends his garden. He points to a producer who’s passing by on the road and exclaims, “See him? He’s rich. He’s a landowner!” Iuri clarifies that the man owns only five hectares of vineyard.

While they may not be rich yet, winemakers in the Mandrolisai are trying to get there by bringing more attention to their region. Gian Nicola Carboni of Cantina Carboni has invested in a large new cellar and tasting room, while making significant improvements to his winemaking process. Still using native grapes and methods that result in wines that are very much traditional, he has upgraded. He’s working hard to create the infrastructure required to make the interior of Sardinia as appealing to tourists as its coastline.

Exclusive Access to
Wine Country,
Led by Wine Enthusiast

Carboni brings me to an old aristocratic house he recently acquired and is now renovating into a bed-and-breakfast. The building is filled with dust, hanging wires, and piles of rubble. Among the mess, there is an old cabinet leaning up against the wall. He takes out a bottle and says, “I found a very old white wine of ours in here. Should we open it by the pool?” I have never not wanted to open wine by a pool, I assure him.

We have a lot more than wine waiting for us at the pool, thanks to Chef Daniele Sanna of the restaurant Brasia BBQ Agricolo in Tadasuni. A table is heaving under the weight of hand-cut prosciutto, pecorino ranging from one year to a decade old, veal tartare, fried rice balls stuffed with mutton, and steaks cut thicker than bricks and then waved over the grill for the merest of cooks. Then we open the bottle from the cabinet.

It is a 20-year-old Nuragus, an indigenous Sardinian grape rarely seen outside the island. What pours from this bottle is textured, substantial, nutty, sweet, and dense—a lot like my favorite of all wines, Marsala, from that more “cultured” destination, Sicily. But unlike Marsala, this Nuragus does not have an expected outcome; it’s a wine that tells the story of how a region finds its footing rather than one that reflects back on how it succeeded. This bottle is a promise for the future, and I am suddenly flushed with hope for the island it came from.

We’re joined by winemaker Massimiliano Mura, the president of the Consorzio here. He leans over to me and says something in the Sardinian dialect that he explains roughly translates to: “We have this vice—we have to make everyone feel at home.” I don’t quite understand what he means, and when I check the phrase later, I realize what he really said was, “We have this vice—we have to make everyone want to live here.”

The slight semantic difference gives me the answer to my question about how Sardinia has stayed off map for so long. All of the best parts of the island and its people are hard to get to know, but they’re not bothered by that. Anyone worthy of discovering it will come and find out for themselves.

Bottles to Look For

Sella & Mosca 2021 Riserva Cannonau (Cannonau di Sardegna)

Big doesn’t have to mean boring, especially not in Sardinia, and Sella&Mosca craft several exciting wines that are widely available all over the world. Their Cannonau remains a benchmark— juicy and rich, but never heavy or tiring. A true island red that shines with a light chill. 91 Points.

$18 Total Wine

Antichi Poderi Jerzu 2021 Filare Vermentino (Vermentino di Sardegna)

Plums, black cherries, figs, top soil, gun flint on the nose perfectly foreshadow a palate which is simultaneously earthy with herbs and more soil, and dense with dark but still snappy fruit. The finish brings notes of dark chocolate and espresso, as well as a lingering heat.

Inquire with the winery

Cantina Fradiles 2021 Angraris (Mandrolisai)

With 10 whole hectares in the Mandrolisai, Cantina Fradiles counts as a giant of the subregion, even if it manages a tiny fraction of the vineyards its neighbors have. Their Mandrolisai DOC Superiore comes from the three native red grapes—Cannonau, Bovale, and Monica— grown in an ancient vineyard, worked by hand with traditional practices. The result is expansive, dense, and profound.

Inquire with the winery

VikeVike 2024 Bianco Granazza (Barbagia)

The Wine Enthusiast Italian team’s newest Avenger, Michael Laudenslager, also took a trip through Sardinia this year and reported back from the Mamoiada region brimming with excitement over Simone Sedilesu’s spirited expression of Granazza, a native white grape. He also suggests keeping an eye out for Sedilesu’s wife Federica Dessolis’s ‘Esole’ wines, which are en route to the U.S. and informed by her time at a little winery called Domaine Romanée-Conti.

$63 Leon & Son

Tenute Gregu di Raffaele Gregu 2022 Pitraia Vermentino (Vermentino di Gallura)

An immensely charming property set in the northern subregion of Gallura, Tenute Gregu is still breaking into the U.S. market. In the meantime, visit the property to sit under their pergola, try the food made by Raffaele Gregu’s partner, Marianna Pinna, and taste the Gregu brothers’ botrytized Vermentino, aged 18 months on the lees. It’s totally unexpected, delicious, and weird—what could be more Sardinian? 92 Points.

Inquire with the winery

Foods of Sardinia

Some Sardinian words are pre-Indo-European in origin, and the island’s dialects are radically different from standard Italian. Linguists study Sardinian for clues to unravel ancient migration patterns across the Mediterranean. While there’s little hope for most of us outsiders to ever fully grasp their language, learning about the local food is a very satisfying way to pick up some Sardo while eating and drinking phenomenally well.

Bread

In Sardinia, instead of leavened wheat loaves you’ll find ultrathin, cracker-crisp rounds known as pane carasau accompanying every meal. When seasoned with olive oil and salt, it’s called pane guttiau.

Pasta

Fregola is a toothy, dense, couscous-adjacent pasta. Malloreddus is shaped like tiny shells and often paired with sausage. Culurgiones are elegant, oval-shaped, finger-pinched dumplings usually filled with pecorino, potatoes, and fresh mint.

Bottarga

While pressed, dried roe of mullet or cod is not exclusive to the island, Sardinians have a borderline toxic relationship with the salty, umami intensity it imparts. Liberal dustings of bottarga are used as a condiment for many pasta dishes, and you’ll even find it sliced and tossed with celery for those who feel it deserves center stage.

Sheep

Sheep play a major role in Sardinian life, and every product from the animal is a part of the culinary landscape, from the various cheeses derived from its milk (all some form of pecorino) and the staple dish agnello in umido (stewed lamb) to the long-cooked cuts of mutton that are used as stuffing in rice balls and dumplings.

Pork

Sardinians know how to cook a pig, and an occasion isn’t truly important if porceddu doesn’t make an appearance. Crackling skin over a thick layer of fat envelops the sweet, nutty meat in a symphony of flavors that can provoke tears of gluttonous joy.

Blood

Whatever animal is being slaughtered, one thing is certain: Sardinians will not let a single part go to waste, and that includes the blood. It can be sweetened with sugar and spices, thickened and poured over rice, and coagulated and rolled into sausage. Pig and sheep’s blood dishes like sambeneddu, zurrette, and sanguinaccio are classics, even if they are not appreciated by everyone.

Seadas

A plate of seadas or sebadas will inevitably land in front of you, and I have yet to meet the person who can resist this incredible combination of sweet, melted cheese tucked inside half moons of fried pastry and doused with honey and powdered sugar. True connoisseurs know to ask for Corbezzolo honey, which is made by bees that pollinate the Sardinian strawberry tree.

Mirto

The coup de grâce of any Sardinian meal is a dense, violet-hued digestif known as mirto. The island’s signature spirit is derived from the ancient myrtle plant, and the best versions of it are fiery, fierce, and made at home.
